changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:06 June 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise reconsider your need to travel to Nigeria due to the threat of terrorism, kidnapping, violent crime and the risk of civil unrest. Higher levels apply in some areas. We now advise do not travel to Kwara State, in addition to a number of other states in Nigeria. There's a high risk of terrorist attacks across Nige

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Higher levels apply in some areas.
We now advise do not travel to Kwara State, in addition to a number of other states in Nigeria.
There's a high risk of terrorist attacks across Nigeria.
Terrorist attacks may be more likely around religious holidays and other significant dates.
Always be alert to threats, especially in public places.

sections.heading: Safety
added: There's a high risk of terrorist attacks across Nigeria by various militant groups.
Attacks could be indiscriminate or may target foreign interests.
Terrorist attacks may be more likely around religious holidays and other significant dates.

Potential targets include places where crowds gather, such as government buildings, law enforcement and military facilities, hotels, bars, restaurants, political meetings, places of worship, schools, markets, shopping malls, sporting events, transport hubs and
Avoid all demonstrations, strikes, rallies and large gatherings.

sections.heading: Health
added: Other insect-borne diseases include malaria and dengue.
Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof.
Consider taking anti-malarial medication.
Check your vaccinations are up to date before you travel.
Poliovirus outbreaks have occurred in Nigeria in the last 12 months (Circulating Vaccine Derived Poliovirus Type 2).

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences include long prison sentences and large fines.
Serious crimes, such as murder and armed robbery, can attract the death penalty.
Nigeria has strict laws against same-sex relationships.
Avoid public displays of affection and adhere to conservative dress codes.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You'll need a visa to travel to Nigeria.
You can apply and pay for a visa online on the Nigeria Immigration Service website.
Complete landing and departure cards online before entering or exiting the country.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
Contact the nearest embassy, high commission or consulate of Nigeria for details about visas, customs and quarantine rules.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission’s social media accounts.
The Australian High Commission in Abuja and Australia’s Honorary Consulate in Lagos provide limited consular and passport services.
If you travel to 'do not travel' areas despite our advice, we may not be able to provide you with consular services.

sections.heading: Security situation
added: We continue to advise reconsider your need to travel due to the threat of terrorism, kidnapping, violent crime and the risk of civil unrest.
Higher levels apply in some areas.
We also advise do not travel to Kwara State, in addition to a number of other 'do not travel' states in Nigeria.
If you travel to Nigeria, you're at risk of: terrorist attack kidnapping civil unrest crime The security situation could change suddenly.
You could get caught up in violence directed at others.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: There's a high risk of terrorist attacks in Nigeria by various militant groups.
Further attacks are highly likely.
If you're in a 'do not travel' area, leave if it's safe to do so.

Terrorist attacks: can happen anywhere and at any time may specifically target religious and public holidays may be indiscriminate may target venues frequently visited by foreign nationals Potential targets may include: hotels, bars, restaurants, markets and s
Australian officials rarely travel outside of Abuja and Lagos, due to the high-risk security environment.

If you're a dual national of a European country, check whether the EES a

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Depending on why you're travelling and how long you plan to stay, you may require a visa for travel to the Schengen area.
Check the European Entry and Exit (EES) and visa rules before you travel.
If you're a dual national of a European country, check whether the EES applies to you and what passport you should enter on.
There may be delays at border crossings (see ‘Travel’).
There's an ongoing threat of terrorism in Germany.

sections.heading: Overview
added: The situation in the Middle East remains unpredictable.
Military conflict in the Middle East region may cause travel disruptions and your travel plans may be affected even if your destination is not the Middle East.
‘Reconsider your need to travel’ also means ‘reconsider your need to transit’.
If you need to transit any level 3 locations, stay as short a time as possible and eliminate unnecessary activities.
In Europe, prioritise your safety, monitor developments closely and follow local authorities’ advice, including any warnings to avoid areas deemed to be at high risk of targeting.

sections.heading: Safety
added: There's an ongoing threat of terrorism.
Terrorists have staged attacks and threatened more.
They may target transport hubs and places foreigners visit, including Christmas markets.
Attacks could occur anywhere at any time.
There have been multiple attacks using vehicles or knives in recent years.

sections.heading: Health
added: Take care in forests and parks, including urban areas, as there is a risk of tick‑borne encephalitis.
Ticks are especially active from March to November.
Check for ticks and remove them as soon as possible.
Medical care and facilities are of a high standard.
If you don't have insurance, hospitals may ask for up-front payment.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Nazi symbols, salutes, songs or material, such as flags or memorabilia, are all illegal.
Full travel advice: Local laws

sections.heading: Travel
added: Germany is a part of the Schengen area which means you can enter Germany without a visa in some cases.
In other situations, you'll need a visa.

If you’re travelling to a country in the Schengen area for a short stay (90 days or less) using an Australian passport, you’ll be required to register your biometric details, such as fingerprints and a photo, when you arrive.
You don’t need to take any action before you arrive at the border, and there is no cost for EES registration.
There may be delays at border crossings.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Berlin or Consulate-General in Frankfurt.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the embassy’s social media accounts.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorism is a threat worldwide.
Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time.
Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places.
Germany maintains strong security measures, including at airports and major train stations and authorities continue to disrupt plots and arrest suspected terrorists.
Germany has experienced several attacks in recent years, including knife and vehicle assaults.

Protests are common and general strikes happen sometimes. Strik

sections.heading: Latest update
added: We've reviewed our travel advice and we've lowered the level of our advice.
We now advise exercise a high degree of caution overall due to the risk of civil unrest and natural disasters.
Protests are common and general strikes happen sometimes.
Strikes can disrupt essential services.
Don't visit government buildings during political unrest or use a vehicle during strikes as it may be a target.

sections.heading: Tourist Police
added: Call : 1144: headquarters 01-424704: Kathmandu 061-52761: Pokhara, Kaski 069-410033: Ghorepani, Myagdi 069-440375: Mustang (Jomsom) 066-440313: Chame, Manang Latest update Following the civil unrest in 2025, the security situation in Nepal has stabilised.
We've reviewed our travel advice and we've lowered the level of our advice.
We now advise exercise a high degree of caution overall due to the risk of civil unrest and natural disasters.
Protests are common and general strikes happen sometimes.
Strikes can disrupt essential services.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Protests are common and general strikes occasionally occur.
Strikes can disrupt essential services.
Don't visit government buildings during political unrest or use a vehicle during strikes as it may be a target.
Don't participate in public protests as it would breach your visa conditions and you could be arrested and deported.
Travellers have been victims of armed robberies and assaults, including sexual assaults.

sections.heading: Health
added: Altitude sickness is a risk above 2,500m.
It can be life-threatening.
It can affect anyone, even people who are physically fit.
Ensure your travel insurance covers medical evacuation from altitude.
Some areas, including Kathmandu, have high levels of seasonal smog and pollution.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
You may be offered illegal drugs in tourist areas.
Penalties for drug offences are severe, including for possessing small amounts.
Be careful when taking photos.
It's illegal to photograph army barracks, checkpoints and military personnel.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You need a visa to enter Nepal.
Visas on arrival are available, but you should check with the nearest Nepal Embassy for details and conditions.
Nepal issues trekking and mountaineering permits, but check with your intended tour provider for details.
The local currency is the Nepalese Rupee (NPR).
Exchange foreign currency for NPR at banks and exchange bureaus in major centres.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy's social media accounts.
For consular assistance, contact the Australian Embassy in Kathmandu.

sections.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
added: Calls for protests, strikes and demonstrations sometimes occur.
Violent clashes between protesters and authorities can occur.
Avoid visiting government buildings during political unrest.

During protests, strikes, demonstrations or political unrest: stay alert limit your movements and don't go out unless you have to avoid areas affected by demonstrations, protests and other public gatherings check local media for new threats, especially when po
If you lose contact with a relative or friend who's in Nepal, try alternative means such as phone, text, email or other social media platforms such as Viber.

changes.updateNote: New information about attending festivals and concerts and using swimming pools and updated information about protecting yourself and your belongings, and beach safety (‘Safety and security’ page).

sections.heading: Staying longer than 90 days in a 180-day period
added: This does not apply to you if you’re the holder of a residence permit or long stay visa.

sections.heading: Protecting yourself and your belongings
added: Be alert to the risk of street crime.
Take care on public transport and at crowded bus and tram stops.
Thieves use distraction techniques and often work in teams.
Look after your passport, money and personal belongings when travelling, checking in luggage at the airport or arranging car hire.
Do not carry all your valuables in one place.

sections.heading: Attacks and sexual assault
added: Attacks including sexual assaults, are rare but do occur.
The risk is highest late at night around popular nightlife locations.
In an emergency, call 112.
Avoid splitting up from your friends, do not go off with people you don’t know and don’t let a friend walk back to their accommodation alone.
Set up a group chat to keep in touch.

sections.heading: Drink spiking and alcohol
added: Be alert to the possible use of ‘date rape’ and other drugs including GHB and liquid ecstasy.
Buy your own drinks and always keep them in sight to avoid them being spiked.
Alcohol and drugs can reduce your vigilance, making you less in control.
If you drink, know your limit.
Drinks served in bars in Portugal are often stronger than those in the UK.

sections.heading: Local laws governing public behaviour in Albufeira
Festivals, concerts and cultural events
added: Many large events are held in Portugal each year.
Follow the advice of police and local authorities when attending events.
Take care of your valuables if attending a festival or large concert.
See the festival travel checklist for more advice.

sections.heading: Swimming pools
added: Always supervise children.
Keep small children within arms-reach in and around swimming pools, even if they can swim or there is a lifeguard present.
Be particularly vigilant of children if you are staying in a villa with a private pool.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Portugal's national terrorism alert is 'significant' (level 3 out of 5).
Always be alert to terrorism.
Terrorists have targeted European cities, including transport hubs and places visited by travellers.
Take official warnings seriously.
Strikes and public demonstrations are common.

sections.heading: Health
added: The Portuguese health system is experiencing ongoing staffing shortages and strikes.
You may experience delays in accessing public health care services, particularly for non-urgent care.
Large private medical facilities in major cities are comparable to Australia's standards.
Standards in public facilities and rural areas vary but are generally of a lower standard than in Australia.
Private medical facilities provide good quality care but may require payment upfront.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Judicial procedures can be lengthy and are often subject to delays.
Seek advice from a local lawyer or Portugal's Victims of Crime support unit (APAV).
Gambling is legal in official casinos and licensed venues.
It's illegal everywhere else.
Check that you're entering a licensed venue.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Portugal is a part of the Schengen area which means you can enter Portugal without a visa in some cases.
In other situations, you'll need a visa.

If you’re travelling to a country in the Schengen area for a short stay (90 days or less) using an Australian passport, you’ll be required to register your biometric details, such as fingerprints and a photo, when you arrive.
You don’t need to take any action before you arrive at the border, and there is no cost for EES registration.
There may be delays at border crossings.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
If you're a victim of crime, get support from Portugal's Victims of Crime support unit, APAV.
English-speaking services are available.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Bag snatching, pickpocketing and theft from cars and accommodation can occur, especially in tourist areas.

Pay particular attention to your belongings at: tourist attractions and museums railway stations and cruise terminals public transport, especially on trams in heavily frequented tourist areas bars, nightclubs, restaurants and hotel foyers beaches accommodation
Pay attention to your belongings when leaving cruise ships or transferring to buses and local transport.

To keep your valuables safe: keep your passport and other identity documents separate use ATMs in banks, shops and shopping centres, not on the street don't leave your bag on the back of the chair or at your feet in restaurants or cafes store valuables in a se
Put a card with your contact details in your wallet.

sections.heading: Theft from vehicles
added: Don't agree to compensate drivers for alleged damage to their vehicles.
Always contact the police.
Thieves target vehicles with foreign licence plates and rental car stickers.

To protect your belongings in your vehicle: keep car doors locked and luggage out of sight at all times use a secure parking facility, especially overnight don't leave your vehicle unattended for long periods avoid hiring cars with rental company stickers.

Violent crime is widespread. Carjackers and armed robbers may target people travelling to and from the airport or at traffic lights. Arrive and depart the airport in daylight. Always keep vehicle doors and windows locked. Don't

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Violent crime is widespread.
Carjackers and armed robbers may target people travelling to and from the airport or at traffic lights.
Arrive and depart the airport in daylight.
Always keep vehicle doors and windows locked.
Don't walk outside or travel after dark (see 'Safety').

sections.heading: Safety
added: A State of Exception is periodically declared in some municipalities in Honduras.
When there's a State of Exception, there may be restrictions on freedom of movement and other rights.
Monitor local media for updates and information on restrictions in specific neighbourhoods.
You must always carry identification and comply with all local laws and police instructions.
Violent crime is widespread.

sections.heading: Health
added: Zika virus is widespread.
If you're pregnant, discuss this with your doctor.
Malaria is a risk throughout Honduras, including the Bay Islands.
This increases during the rainy season from May to November.
Consider taking anti-malaria medication.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences are severe and include long prison sentences.
Understand the rules on exporting and importing.
It's illegal to import religious materials.
It's also illegal to export firearms, pre-colonial antiques or artefacts.

sections.heading: Travel
added: To enter Honduras, you must purchase a tourist card at the airport.
This allows you to visit Honduras for up to 90 days.
If you're not a tourist or you plan to stay longer, you'll need a visa.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Honduras for the latest details.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Australia has a consulate in Tegucigalpa, headed by an honorary consul.
This consulate provides limited consular assistance to Australians in Honduras.

sections.heading: Violent crime
added: Violent crime is widespread in Honduras.
This includes: murder robbery burglary kidnapping carjacking sexual assault Crime often involves firearms and street gangs.
There have been mass shootings in urban centres.
Honduras has very high rates of murder.
Travellers have been sexually assaulted, often following spiking of food or drink.

sections.heading: Petty crime
added: Petty crime, like pickpocketing and bag-snatching, is common.
You're at risk: in airports at hotels in bus terminals in shopping malls at other crowded public venues You may be robbed at an ATM.
To reduce your risks when withdrawing or changing money: use ATMs in daylight hours withdraw or change money in secure areas like hotels, banks or shopping centres Report problems to the police.
If you're attacked or robbed, don't resist.
If criminals have guns or weapons, they may injure or kill you.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Violent and petty crime is common in Lesotho.
Gun-related crimes and break-ins have targeted travellers.
Security risks increase at night and on weekends.
Avoid walking alone or at night and pay close attention to your personal security.
If you're a victim of violent crime, including sexual assault, seek immediate medical help.

sections.heading: Health
added: The rate of HIV/AIDS infection is very high.
Take precautions if you engage in any high-risk activities.
Waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases are common.
Drink bottled water with intact seals.
Boil water that is not from a sealed container.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Penalties for drug offences are severe and include long prison sentences.
If you're detained or arrested, local authorities may not notify the Australian Government.
Ask police or prison officials to contact the Australian High Commission in Pretoria, South Africa if you're arrested or detained.
Serious crimes, such as murder and sexual assault, can attract the death penalty.
Same-sex relationships are not criminalised.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Tourists can enter Lesotho for up to 30 days without a visa.
If you're travelling from a country where yellow fever occurs, you'll need a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Lesotho.
The local currency is the Lesotho Loti (LSL).
South African Rand (ZAR) is widely accepted.
Use reputable and professional guides or tour operators.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Lesotho.
For consular help, contact the Australian High Commission in Pretoria.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Violent and petty crime is common in Lesotho, particularly in Maseru, and can include: muggings and armed robbery carjacking pickpocketing Police resources are varied throughout Lesotho.
Gun-related crimes and break-ins have targeted travellers.
Security risks increase at night and on weekends.

To protect yourself from violent crime: avoid walking alone or at night keep valuables out of sight always keep car doors locked and windows up, including when moving use ATMs in controlled areas such as banks, shops and shopping centres always keep your credi
Please contact the Consular Emergency Centre or the Australian High Commission in Pretoria for assistance.

sections.heading: Kidnapping
added: Kidnapping can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe.
The Australian Government's longstanding policy is that it doesn't make payments or concessions to kidnappers.
More information, including advice on reducing your risk of being kidnapped: Kidnapping

sections.heading: Safety
added: Protests can lead to violent clashes, including the use of deadly force.
Petty crime is common and violent crime can occur.
Safety risks are greater at night, along roads and in rural areas.
Avoid walking alone, particularly at night and in isolated areas.
Carjacking is reported along major routes from South Africa and Mozambique, especially at night.

sections.heading: Health
added: The standard of medical facilities is poor.

Malaria occurs year-round in most areas.

added: Serious offences, including murder and treason, carry the death penalty.

Penalties for drug offences are severe.

sections.heading: Travel

sections.heading: Crime
added: Petty crime is common in major towns, particularly Mbabane and Manzini.
This includes pickpocketing and robbery.
Be particularly careful in major towns.
Stay alert and be aware of your surroundings, especially if you’re out after dark.
Violent crime, including armed assault and carjacking, can occur.

sections.heading: Advisory summary
added: government employees working in El Salvador are allowed to travel throughout the country during daylight hours.
However, due to road conditions, hilly terrain, possible landslides during the rainy season, and general lack of streetlights, U.S.
government employees are prohibited from traveling between cities or departments (the Salvadoran equivalent of states) at night.
Gang activity has decreased dramatically since 2022, causing a drop in violent crime and murders.

sections.heading: If you decide to travel to El Salvador:
added: Enroll in the Smart Traveler Enrollment Program (STEP) to get important updates and alerts from the U.S.
embassy or consulate contact you or your emergency contact in an emergency.

Whether you’re a first time or frequent traveler, use the International Travel Checklist.

sections.heading: About El Salvador
added: El Salvador is located in Central America, in the Western Hemisphere.
It has maritime borders with the Pacific Ocean.
It also shares land borders with Guatemala and Honduras.
It is slightly smaller than Massachusetts.
There is 1 time zone, Central Standard Time.

sections.heading: Tourist visa requirements
added: No visa is required for stays of 90 days or less.

sections.heading: Vaccinations
added: Yellow fever: Required if arriving from certain countries in South America or Africa.

The following vaccinations are recommended by the CDC: Measles, mumps, rubella, tetanus, pertussis, and chickenpox: These diseases are more common in El Salvador than in the United States, especially among children.
Hepatitis A and typhoid: Immunization is recommended for all travelers.
Hepatitis B: Vaccination is recommended for all those who may have sexual contact, tattoos, or require medical treatment while in El Salvador.
Yellow fever: Travelers coming from countries where yellow fever is endemic, such as Panama, must have had a yellow fever vaccination to enter the country.

sections.heading: Valid passport requirements
added: Your passport must be valid at the time of entry.

sections.heading: Latest update
added: If you’re coming from the DRC, you may be denied entry or subjected to mandatory quarantine measures (see ‘Travel’).
We continue to advise exercise normal safety precautions overall in Rwanda.
Higher levels apply in some areas.
Fighting in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) may affect people on the Rwandan side of the border.
Do not travel to the Rubavu district, including the town of Gisenyi, due to the volatile security situation.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Do not travel to the Rubavu district, including the town of Gisenyi, due to the volatile security situation, which includes the risk of active conflict.
If you're in the Rubavu district, you should leave by commercial means if it is safe to do so.
Reconsider your need to travel within 10 kilometres of the rest of Rwanda’s border with the DRC, including in parts of the Rusizi district, due to the potential of nearby conflict.
The land border between Rwanda and Burundi has been closed since 2024.
Border crossings between Rwanda and DRC could close without notice.

sections.heading: Health
added: Medical facilities are very limited.
If you're ill or injured, you may need medical evacuation to Kenya.
Check your travel insurance covers this.
Outbreaks of Ebola disease have occurred in parts of Africa, including in the neighbouring country of the DRC.
The Rwandan Ministry of Health has increased measures for travellers arriving from the DRC that may be affected by Ebola.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties include long prison sentences and heavy fines.
Same-sex relations are legal.
However, LGBTQIA+ travellers may be harassed by the public and police.
Avoid public displays of affection.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Australians can get a free 30-day tourist visa on arrival.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact the nearest high commission/embassy or consulate of Rwanda for the latest details.
If you're arriving in Rwanda from an Ebola affected area, you'll be required to undergo thermal scanner screening.
Border crossings between the DRC and Rwanda have closed to most travellers due to an outbreak of Ebola disease.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas The Canadian High Commission in Kigali provides limited consular assistance to Australians in Rwanda.
The High Commission doesn't issue passports or provide notarial services.
Full consular assistance is available from the Australian High Commission in Kenya.

sections.heading: Border areas
added: There's active fighting near the Rwandan border in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), including shelling and reports of gunfire in Gisenyi.
Do not travel to the Rubavu district, including the town of Gisenyi, due to the volatile security situation.
If you're in the Rubavu district, you should leave by commercial means if it is safe to do so.
We advise do not travel to the DRC.
Border crossings between the DRC and Rwanda have closed to most travellers due to an outbreak of Ebola disease.

sections.heading: Violence and crime
added: Grenade attacks and other violence have occurred in the past, including in Kigali.
People have been killed and injured in these attacks.

Targets for attacks have included: genocide memorial sites markets taxi and bus stops To protect yourself from attacks: be alert to any security threats, especially around common targets monitor the media and other sources for possible threats follow the advic
Stay alert during daylight hours.

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:10 June 2026 Latest update:You must complete an electronic arrival card to enter China. Never share personal information unless you are certain of the caller’s identity. If you'r

sections.heading: Latest update
added: You can do this before you arrive or at your point of entry (see 'Travel').
Scammers are impersonating trusted phone numbers, including those of the Australian Embassy and consulates.
Never share personal information unless you are certain of the caller’s identity.
If you're unsure, hang up.
Contact the organisation to check if calls are genuine before sharing personal details (see 'Safety').

sections.heading: Safety
added: Criminals target travellers in crowded areas, including on public transport.
Resisting can lead to violence.
Scams targeting travellers can lead to loss of money, violence and serious assault.
Report scams to the nearest police station.
Scammers are impersonating trusted phone numbers, including those of the Australian Embassy and consulates.

sections.heading: Health
added: Tap water may not be safe to drink.
Drink only bottled water with intact seals.
There are risks of mosquito-borne illnesses, including dengue fever and chikungunya.
Protect yourself against mosquito bites.
People have died from bird flu in China.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: The Australian Government can't intervene in the Chinese justice process.
If local authorities consider you a citizen of China, they may refuse to grant you access to Australian consular services.
This will prevent the Australian Government from providing you with those services.
This includes if you're a dual national and don't enter China on your Australian passport.
China has strict laws on national security.

sections.heading: Travel
added: China has included Australia in its visa waiver program.
If you hold an ordinary Australian passport, you may be able to enter China without a visa for no more than 30 days for business, tourism, visiting family and friends or transit.
Chinese authorities will assess your eligibility to enter China.
If authorities consider you're an ineligible or undesirable traveller, you may be questioned by immigration officials and refused entry.
If refused entry, you may be held at your port of arrival while Chinese authorities arrange for your return to Australia (or the last country you visited).

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular services in China, contact the Australian Embassy in Beijing or our Consulates-General in Shanghai, Guangzhou, and Chengdu.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy's social media accounts.

sections.heading: Petty crime
added: Petty crime occurs, including: pickpocketing bag-snatching theft of laptops, passports and mobile phones.
If you resist, you can be injured if criminals turn violent.
Criminals target travellers in crowded areas, including on transport.
People have reported assaults and robberies, particularly in areas popular with expats.
This includes bar and shopping precincts in major cities.

sections.heading: Scams
added: There are scams that target travellers.
Scammers invite travellers for a massage, teahouse service, or nearby cafes and bars.
They offer various reasons and may say they wish 'to practise their English'.
Afterwards, they present travellers with an inflated bill.
They won't let the traveller leave until they pay the bill by credit card.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Petty crime, theft from vehicles and highway robberies can happen.
Be careful on public transport, in tourist areas, petrol stations and highway parking areas.
Use secure parking facilities.
Many locals have firearms.
You might be caught in the wrong place.

sections.heading: Health
added: Take care in forests and rural areas where ticks carry encephalitis.
Ticks are active from spring to autumn.
Check your body for ticks and remove them as soon as possible.
Medical facilities in Bosnia and Herzegovina may not be up to the same standard as those in Australia.
Check your insurance covers medical evacuation.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: It's illegal to take photos of military and police personnel, buildings, vehicles or equipment.
Bosnia and Herzegovina recognises dual nationality only in certain circumstances.
This limits the consular help we can give.
Always travel on your Australian passport.
Same-sex relationships are legal but aren't widely accepted.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorism is a threat worldwide.
Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time.
Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places.

While there have been no recent terrorist attacks, possible targets include: public transport, including train stations and airports hotels, clubs, restaurants and cafes government buildings, including schools places of worship or religious holidays public are

sections.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
added: Public protests and events that draw large groups of people can turn violent.
Ethnic and religious tensions may result in demonstrations or conflict.
Incidents can happen with little or no warning and may target foreigners.
To stay safe during periods of unrest: avoid all protests and demonstrations monitor the media for updates follow the advice of local authorities More information: Protests and civil unrest

Authorities can request access to personal electronic devices and passwords from anyone in Hong Kong, including if you’re tran

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Hong Kong has strict laws on national security that can be interpreted broadly and could be applied to activities that occurred overseas.
Authorities can request access to personal electronic devices and passwords from anyone in Hong Kong, including if you’re transiting through the airport.

From 30 April, it's illegal to import alternative smoking products (vapes, heated tobacco products, herbal cigarettes) or possess them in public places, even in transit inside the airport (see 'Local laws').
We advise: Exercise a high degree of caution in Hong Kong.
Download Hong Kong Download Asia

sections.heading: Safety
added: Hong Kong has strict laws on national security that can be interpreted broadly.
You could be detained without charge and be denied access to a lawyer.
The Hong Kong Government strictly controls demonstrations.
It's illegal to participate in public demonstrations without the approval of the government.
Authorities may arrest protesters.

sections.heading: Health
added: It's illegal to carry sleeping tablets and some medication without a prescription.
Carry a copy of your prescription or a letter from your doctor.
Hong Kong can have very high pollution levels and has very high humidity from May to October.
If you have a heart or breathing condition, minimise physical and outdoor activities when the air quality index health risk is high and on very humid days.
Hand, foot and mouth disease (HFMD) is common.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: The 2024 Safeguarding National Security Law and the 2020 National Security Law may be interpreted broadly.
The laws could be applied to activities outside Hong Kong, including social media posts.
Hong Kong authorities can request access to personal devices and passwords from anyone in Hong Kong.
You could break the law without intending to.
You may be at increased risk of detention.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Hong Kong has separate immigration regulations to mainland China.
You generally won't need a visa if you intend to be a visitor for less than 90 days.
Contact the Hong Kong Economic and Trade Office in Sydney for details.
If you plan to return to mainland China after visiting Hong Kong, confirm your Chinese visa allows you to re-enter mainland China.
See the Embassy of the People's Republic of China for eligibility requirements and visa types.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.

Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.

To stay up to date with local information, follow the Consulate-General's social media accounts.

sections.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
added: Public protests and events that draw large groups of people can turn violent.
The Hong Kong Government prohibits demonstrations that they haven't approved.
Authorities may arrest protesters.
Avoid protests, demonstrations and large gatherings.
Don't photograph, film or participate in protests or other acts that authorities may consider provocative.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorism is a threat worldwide.
Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time.
Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places.
If there’s a terrorist incident, leave the area if it’s safe to do so and follow the instructions of local authorities.
More information: Terrorism

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:09 April 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise exercise normal safety precautions in Japan. Following legislative changes to family law, joint child custody after divorce is now possible in some cases. If you're involved in a dispute over a child or other family matter disputes, it is important to seek legal advice about your options both in Japan and in Au

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Following legislative changes to family law, joint child custody after divorce is now possible in some cases.
If you're involved in a dispute over a child or other family matter disputes, it is important to seek legal advice about your options both in Japan and in Australia (see 'Local laws').
Australians are eligible for Japan's visa exemption scheme for short-stay tourism and business travel.
Currently, you don't need a visa to travel to Japan for up to 90 days but entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
It is your responsibility to verify visa requirements from the nearest embassy or consulate of Japan.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Japan is prone to earthquakes, typhoons and tsunamis.
Japanese authorities have detailed plans to respond to natural disasters.
In an emergency, follow the advice of local authorities and emergency signage.
Move to higher ground immediately if you're in a coastal region after a major earthquake.
Check the Disaster Prevention Portal for more information.

sections.heading: Health
added: Japan has strict rules about bringing medicine into the country, including some ingredients in ADHD and cold and flu medication.
If you plan on bringing in medication, you must check if it's legal before you travel.
See the Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare website for more information.
Restricted zones exist around the Fukushima Dai-ichi nuclear power plant.
The 2011 earthquake caused the release of lethal radiation.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Japan has strict alcohol laws.
The legal drinking age is 20.
It's illegal to drive with any alcohol in your bloodstream.
Allowing someone who has been drinking to drive is also illegal.
Laws restrict alcohol consumption in specific areas on certain days, such as in Shibuya around Halloween (31 October) and New Year's Eve.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Australians are eligible for Japan's visa exemption scheme for short-stay tourism and business travel.
Currently, you don't need a visa to travel to Japan for up to 90 days but entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
It is your responsibility to verify visa requirements from the nearest embassy or consulate of Japan.
If you're travelling for any other reason, contact your nearest Japanese embassy or consulate to check if you need a visa, especially if you plan to work in Japan.
Penalties may apply if you work in Japan on a tourist visa.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Tokyo or the Consulate-General in Osaka.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy’s social media accounts Full travel advice: Local contacts

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorism is a threat worldwide.
Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time.
Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places.

Japan has security measures in place at key facilities, such as: public transport public event venues entry ports If there’s a terrorist incident, leave the area if it’s safe to do so and follow the instructions of local authorities.
More information: Terrorism

sections.heading: Crime
added: Japan has a low crime rate.
Even so, some petty theft can happen, like bag snatching at popular tourist attractions.

There's a risk of crime in bars and nightclubs, especially in Shinjuku (Kabuki-cho), Shibuya and Roppongi entertainment areas of Tokyo, and the Shinsaibashi, Namba and Dotonbori entertainment areas of Osaka.
Both men and women have been targeted.

You may encounter: overcharging fraudulent credit card charges forced withdrawal of large amounts of cash at ATMs drink spiking illegal drugs assault You may be served drinks with higher alcohol content than normal.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Pickpocketing and bag snatching is common, especially on public transport and in crowded areas.
Criminals posing as police have robbed travellers in the Sukhbaatar Square area of Ulaanbaatar.
Be alert to thieves, especially on public transport.
While uncommon, violent crime is present in Mongolia, particularly in Ulaanbaatar.

sections.heading: Health
added: The standard of medical care is poor, particularly outside Ulaanbaatar.
Bring basic medical supplies with you.
You'll have to pay up-front, even in an emergency.
If you're seriously ill or injured, you'll need medical evacuation.
Ensure your travel insurance covers this.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.

If you live in Mongolia, also carry your residency card.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Australian tourists can enter Mongolia for stays of up to 30 days without a visa.

Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.

sections.heading: Latest update
added: If you decide to remain, you're responsible for your own safety.
Our ability to provide consular services in Somalia is extremely limited.
Terrorist attacks occur frequently.
The UK and US Embassies in Somalia issue frequent security alerts about potential imminent terrorist attacks (see 'Safety').
We continue to advise: Do not travel to Somalia due to the dangerous security situation and the threat of armed conflict, terrorism, kidnapping and violent crime.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Do not travel to Somalia.
If you're in the country, leave as soon as possible.
If you decide to stay, you're responsible for your own safety.
Get professional security advice.
Our ability to provide consular services is severely limited.

sections.heading: Health
added: Make sure you get vaccinated against yellow fever, polio, diphtheria and other illnesses before you travel.
Ensure your vaccinations are up to date.
Carry proof of vaccination when you travel.
Outbreaks of Ebola disease have occurred in parts of Africa.
Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone displaying symptoms.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Courts operate under both Somali and Sharia law.
In areas under al-Shabaab control, sentences include flogging and the death penalty.
Get local legal advice, especially on matters such as sexual assault, divorce, child custody and child support.
Domestic and sexual violence against women remains a serious issue.
There are no laws against spousal violence, including rape.

sections.heading: Travel
added: The Somali Immigration and Citizenship Agency confirmed there was a data breach in November 2025 affecting its e-visa system.
Leaked data from the breach potentially includes visa applicants’ personal details, including names, photos, dates of birth and home addresses.
Australians who have applied for a Somali e-visa may be affected.
If despite our advice you choose to travel, consider the risks before applying for a visa.
You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Somalia for the latest details.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Australia doesn't have a diplomatic mission in Somalia.
For consular help, contact the Australian High Commission in Kenya.
The ability of the Australian Government to provide consular help to Australians in Somalia is severely limited.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorist attacks occur often in Somalia, and can take place anywhere, at any time.
Targets include Somali government officials, foreigners and UN workers.
Attacks have occurred in Mogadishu, Beledweyne, Baidoa, Galkayo and Kismayo and have resulted in hundreds of fatalities and injuries.
Somali militants have threatened attacks throughout the country.
Aden Adde International Airport is a high-priority target because of the large number of foreigners present.

sections.heading: Security
added: Do not travel to Somalia.
If you're already there, leave as soon as possible.
It doesn't have an effective police force.

If, despite our advice, you decide to stay, you should: get professional security advice arrange personal security measures plan for uncertainty and stock up on essential items (water, food, toiletries, fuel and prescription medication) limit your movements, e
leave the area straight away if it's safe to do so avoid the affected area in case of secondary attacks monitor the media for any new or emerging threats Terrorism is a threat worldwide.

added: Other levels apply in some areas.

We continue to advise: Do not travel to Chad overall due to the dangerous security situation and the threat of terrorism, kidnapping and violent crime.

sections.heading: Safety
added: The security situation is dangerous and unpredictable.
If you're in Chad despite our advice, you should consider leaving.
If you decide to remain, have plans in place to keep yourself safe and be ready to leave at short notice.
Airports and borders could close at short notice.
Contact your travel provider or airline for information.

sections.heading: Health
added: If, despite our advice, you travel to Chad, get vaccinated before you travel.
Malaria is widespread year-round.
Other insect-borne diseases include filariasis and African sleeping sickness.
Waterborne, foodborne and infectious diseases include typhoid, cholera, tuberculosis and rabies.
N'Djamena has a private hospital with international-standard facilities.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Chad is a mainly Muslim country with conservative standards.
Dress and behave discreetly.
Foreigners have been arrested for failing to produce identification.
Always carry identification.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Travel throughout Chad is dangerous.
If you intend to travel by road, despite our advice, hire a car and driver from a reputable company.
The Australian Government can't offer advice on the safety of travelling to departure points.
Any travel you take is at your own risk.
If, despite our advice, you travel to Chad, you need to get a visa before you arrive.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: Our ability to provide consular services in Chad is extremely limited.
Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Chad.
For consular assistance, contact the Australian Embassy in Paris.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy’s social media accounts.
There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations.

sections.heading: Security situation
added: If, despite our advice, you travel to Chad, you should remain vigilant.
The security situation throughout Chad remains volatile, with an ongoing risk of violent conflict and civil unrest in all parts of the country.
Movement restrictions could be implemented at short notice.
Fighting between government forces and armed groups can occur anywhere without warning.
The security situation along Chad’s borders can deteriorate.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorist attacks are a significant risk in Chad, including the Lake Chad Basin and along border areas.
They could happen anywhere and at any time.
Armed groups are active across Chad.
Terrorist groups from neighbouring countries could also launch attacks.
There's a heightened threat of terrorist attacks in or around religious sites and during religious festivals.

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Australian tourists travelling on a regular passport can enter Uruguay for up to 90 days without a visa (see 'Travel').
We advise: Exercise normal safety precautions in Uruguay.
Download Uruguay Download Americas

sections.heading: Local emergency contacts
added: Fire and rescue services Call 911 in Montevideo or 02911 outside Montevideo.
Medical emergencies Call 911 in Montevideo or 02911 outside Montevideo.
Police Call 911 in Montevideo or 02911 outside Montevideo.
Latest update We continue to advise exercise normal safety precautions in Uruguay.
Australian tourists travelling on a regular passport can enter Uruguay for up to 90 days without a visa (see 'Travel').

sections.heading: Safety
added: Violent crime, such as assaults, armed robberies and thefts occur throughout the country.
Armed criminals commonly travel in pairs and approach victims on motorcycles.
They have also targeted grocery stores and restaurants, where bystanders have been affected.
Petty crime, such as pickpocketing and bag snatching, occurs in Uruguay, especially in Montevideo.
Avoid using ATMs on the street.

sections.heading: Health
added: Insect-borne diseases such as dengue and zika virus can be a risk in Uruguay.
Make sure you have comprehensive travel insurance.
Medical care is expensive, and you may have to pay upfront, even in an emergency.
Hantavirus is a risk in Uruguay.
It's generally spread through contact with infected rodents.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences are severe.
They include lengthy prison sentences and heavy fines.
Only Uruguayan citizens and permanent residents can legally buy, cultivate, use or carry cannabis (marijuana) – this remains illegal for tourists and other visitors.
Same-sex relationships and marriage are legal in Uruguay.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Uruguay for the latest details.
You can enter Uruguay for up to 90 days without a visa.
You can use your Australian driver's licence to drive in Uruguay for one year after entry.
You must be at least 21 years old to rent a car.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Australia has a Consulate headed by an Honorary Consul in Montevideo.
It provides limited consular assistance and can assist you with a passport application but doesn't issue Australian passports.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Violent crime, such as assaults, armed robberies and thefts occur throughout the country.
Armed criminals commonly travel in pairs and approach victims on motorcycles before demanding personal belongings.
They have also targeted grocery stores and restaurants, where bystanders have been affected.
Street crime occurs in Uruguay, particularly in Montevideo.
This includes: pickpocketing bag snatching petty theft robberies Smash and grab robberies from cars stopped at traffic lights also occur.

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:29 May 2026 Latest update:Beaches in French Polynesia are not patrolled. There may be strong currents, even in the lagoons. Ask locals before going swimming, obey warning signs and follow the advice of local authorities. Stray dogs are common. Dog attacks have occurred. Don’t approach or touch dogs (see 'Safety'). We advise: Exercise normal safety precautions

sections.heading: Latest update
added: There may be strong currents, even in the lagoons.
Ask locals before going swimming, obey warning signs and follow the advice of local authorities.
Dog attacks have occurred.
Don’t approach or touch dogs (see 'Safety').
We advise: Exercise normal safety precautions in French Polynesia.

sections.heading: Safety
added: The rate of serious crime is low.
But petty crime, including theft, does occur.
Take arranged transport and avoid walking after dark.
Keep your belongings close, especially in crowded places or on the beach.
Don't leave food or drink unattended.

sections.heading: Health
added: Dengue-type epidemics are common in French Polynesia.
Other insect-borne diseases include chikungunya and filariasis.
Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof.
Leptospirosis occurs in French Polynesia.
Wear closed-toed shoes when walking and avoid contact with rivers or muddy water.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Understand the local laws.
French Polynesia is an overseas territory of France.
A mix of French and local laws apply.
Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
You could face fines or imprisonment for having even small amounts.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You likely won't need a visa for a tourist visit of up to 3 months.
However, the total length of your stay must not exceed a total of 90 days over a period of 180 days (6 months).
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
Contact the Consulate-General of France in Sydney or the Embassy of France for the latest details.
Travel insurance is highly advised in French Polynesia and is often mandatory for tour groups and cruise ships.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Australia has a Consulate-General in Papeete, on the island of Tahiti.
They can provide consular assistance and passport services.

sections.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
added: Public protests and events that draw large groups of people can turn violent.
Strikes and industrial disputes can lead to social unrest.
They may disrupt essential services, including: local transport domestic and international flights Avoid demonstrations and crowds.

If there's a strike: check on your flights before going to the airport ask your tour operator if it affects tourist services follow the instructions of local authorities More information: Demonstrations and civil unrest

sections.heading: Crime
added: The rate of serious crime is low in French Polynesia, however assaults can occur.
Petty crime happens, including drink spiking and theft.

changes.description: Advies bijgewerkt door de bron (2025-11-11 → 2026-07-09).

sections.heading: Notas importantes
added: SE RECOMIENDA VIAJAR CON PRECAUCIÓN..
Lea con atención el resto de estas recomendaciones de viaje.
España dispone de Consulados Generales en Casablanca, Agadir, Nador, Larache, Rabat (que incluye Fez y Meknés), Tánger y Tetuán, además de Consulados Honorarios en Marrakech, Fez y Essaouira.

Puede obtener más información a este respecto en este enlace En casos de emergencia grave fuera del horario de oficina, puede llamar al teléfono de emergencia del Consulado en cuya circunscripción se encuentre.
Este teléfono es de uso exclusivo para ciudadanos españoles y está reservado para atender asuntos de especial gravedad (accidentes, desapariciones, peligro de muerte, etc.).
addedNl: HET WORDT AANBEVOLEN OM VOORZICHTIG TE REIZEN.
Lees de rest van deze reisadviezen aandachtig door.
Spanje heeft algemene consulaten in Casablanca, Agadir, Nador, Larache, Rabat (waartoe ook Fez en Meknes behoren), Tanger en Tetouan, evenals ereconsulaten in Marrakech, Fez en Essaouira.
Meer informatie hierover kunt u krijgen via deze link. Bij ernstige calamiteiten buiten kantooruren kunt u bellen met het noodtelefoonnummer van het consulaat in wiens kiesdistrict u zich bevindt.
Dit telefoonnummer is uitsluitend bestemd voor Spaans staatsburgers en is gereserveerd voor bijzonder ernstige zaken (ongevallen, verdwijningen, levensgevaar, enz.).

sections.heading: Documentación y visados
added: Pasaporte: Para entrar en Marruecos, se exige pasaporte en vigor con un plazo de validez superior a los 6 (seis) meses y que deberá ser válido durante toda la estancia en el país.
Antes de iniciar el viaje se recomienda consultar a su centro de vacunación internacional, así como leer las recomendaciones sanitarias por países del Ministerio de Sanidad en su página web .
addedNl: Paspoort: Om Marokko binnen te komen is een geldig paspoort met een geldigheidsduur van meer dan 6 (zes) maanden vereist en moet tijdens uw verblijf in het land geldig zijn.

Voordat u aan de reis begint, wordt aanbevolen om uw internationale vaccinatiecentrum te raadplegen en de gezondheidsaanbevelingen per land van het Ministerie van Volksgezondheid op haar website te lezen.

sections.heading: Seguridad
added: No obstante, a la luz de la situación internacional actual y, en particular, de la inestabilidad en la región vecina del Sahel, no se puede descartar la posibilidad de algún tipo de agresión terrorista o ataque violento contra intereses y nacionales occidental
En éstas, las zonas cercanas a la frontera con Argelia (sin delimitación visible en su gran mayoría y por ello propensas a ser cruzadas sin advertirlo) pueden resultar especialmente problemáticas.
Zonas de riesgo medio: Es conveniente evitar viajar solo o en grupos pequeños por zonas rurales aisladas, pero en todo caso hacerlo en más de un vehículo y de preferencia todoterreno.

En la cadena montañosa del Rif, alrededor de la zona de Ketama, debe extremarse la precaución por la presencia de personas que, en ocasiones, intentan vender hachís insistentemente a los turistas extranjeros.
Igualmente se recomienda ser prudente respecto de posibles compañías accidentales de desconocidos y recurrir, en la medida de lo posible, a los servicios de guías oficiales registrados.
addedNl: In het licht van de huidige internationale situatie en in het bijzonder de instabiliteit in de aangrenzende Sahelregio kan de mogelijkheid van een vorm van terroristische agressie of gewelddadige aanval op westerse belangen en onderdanen echter niet worden uitgesloten.

In deze gebieden kunnen vooral de gebieden dicht bij de grens met Algerije (de overgrote meerderheid zonder zichtbare afbakening en daarom gemakkelijk zonder waarschuwing te worden overschreden) problematisch zijn.

Gebieden met gemiddeld risico: Het is raadzaam om niet alleen of in kleine groepen door geïsoleerde plattelandsgebieden te reizen, maar doe dit in ieder geval in meer dan één voertuig en bij voorkeur off-road.

In het Rifgebergte, rond het Ketama-gebied, moet uiterste voorzichtigheid in acht worden genomen vanwege de aanwezigheid van mensen die soms aandringend proberen hasj aan buitenlandse toeristen te verkopen.
Het wordt ook aanbevolen voorzichtig te zijn met mogelijk toevallig gezelschap van vreemden en, voor zover mogelijk, een beroep te doen op de diensten van officieel geregistreerde gidsen.

sections.heading: Sanidad
added: Las condiciones sanitarias en Marruecos son, en general, aceptables.
En las principales ciudades se puede recurrir, en caso de necesidad, a clínicas privadas donde hay buenos profesionales formados con frecuencia en España o Francia.
Sin embargo, en ciudades de menor población y, en general, en el medio rural, las instalaciones sanitarias son de un nivel muy inferior, poco dotadas y con personal auxiliar poco cualificado.

Conviene extremar las medidas de higiene, prestando atención a la comida (lavando bien las frutas por ejemplo), bebida (agua embotellada fuera de las grandes ciudades) y alojamiento; los puestos ambulantes donde se sirven comidas y aquellos situados al borde d
Para mayor información sobre cuestiones sanitarias, puede consultar la web del Ministerio de Sanidad y la protección social marroquí, así como la de la Organización Mundial de la salud.
addedNl: De sanitaire omstandigheden in Marokko zijn over het algemeen acceptabel.
In de grote steden kunt u, indien nodig, een beroep doen op privéklinieken waar goede professionals zijn die regelmatig worden opgeleid in Spanje of Frankrijk.

In steden met een kleinere bevolking en, in het algemeen, op het platteland, zijn de gezondheidsvoorzieningen echter van een veel lager niveau, slecht uitgerust en met slecht gekwalificeerd hulppersoneel.

Het is raadzaam om extreme hygiënemaatregelen te nemen, waarbij aandacht wordt besteed aan eten (fruit goed wassen bijvoorbeeld), drinken (flessenwater buiten de grote steden) en accommodatie; de straatstalletjes waar eten wordt geserveerd en de kraampjes aan de rand ervan
Voor meer informatie over gezondheidskwesties kunt u de website van het Marokkaanse ministerie van Volksgezondheid en Sociale Bescherming raadplegen, evenals die van de Wereldgezondheidsorganisatie.

sections.heading: Divisas
added: En la actualidad la cotización es de aproximadamente 1€= 10,80 dirhams, pudiendo oscilar el tipo de cambio en un +-5%.
El dirham no tiene convertibilidad exterior, estando sujeta su importación y exportación al control previo del “Office des Changes".

Respecto a la moneda local, los viajeros están autorizados a llevar consigo un valor no superior a los 2.000 dirhams, pues de lo contrario también deberán declararlo, pues la exportación de moneda local está prohibida.
addedNl: Momenteel bedraagt ​​de prijs ongeveer € 1 = 10,80 dirham, en de wisselkoers kan met +-5% schommelen.
De dirham heeft geen buitenlandse convertibiliteit; de import en export ervan zijn onderworpen aan voorafgaande controle door het “Office des Changes”.
Wat de lokale valuta betreft, mogen reizigers een waarde van niet meer dan 2.000 dirham bij zich dragen, anders moeten ze dit ook aangeven, aangezien de export van lokale valuta verboden is.

sections.heading: Otros
added: SEGURO DE VIAJE Es importante contratar, en origen, un seguro de viaje fiable que incluya asistencia y repatriación sanitaria.

ENTRADA DE VEHÍCULOS La entrada con vehículo en territorio marroquí queda reflejada en la ficha policial del interesado, por lo que es obligatorio salir siempre con el mismo vehículo con el que se entró en el país, salvo que pueda justificarse documentalmente

Si la entrada en Marruecos se hace utilizando un vehículo del que no se es propietario, es necesario estar provisto de un documento de cesión debidamente validado ante la Embajada de Marruecos en Madrid o ante alguno de los Consulados de Marruecos en España.
Si se entra en Marruecos con vehículo éste debe estar asegurado con la máxima cobertura posible.
Si se viaja con vehículo propio o de alquiler, es muy habitual pasar por controles de la Gendarmería Real en carretera.
addedNl: REISVERZEKERING Het is belangrijk om aan de bron een betrouwbare reisverzekering af te sluiten die medische hulp en repatriëring omvat.

BINNENKOMST VAN HET VOERTUIG Het binnenkomen met een voertuig op Marokkaans grondgebied wordt weerspiegeld in het politiedossier van de belanghebbende. Het is dus verplicht om altijd te vertrekken met hetzelfde voertuig waarmee u het land bent binnengekomen, tenzij dit kan worden gedocumenteerd.

Als u Marokko binnenkomt met een voertuig dat niet uw eigendom is, moet u over een overdrachtsdocument beschikken dat naar behoren is gevalideerd bij de Marokkaanse ambassade in Madrid of bij een van de Marokkaanse consulaten in Spanje.
Als u met een voertuig Marokko binnenkomt, moet deze verzekerd zijn met de maximaal mogelijke dekking.
Als u met uw eigen of huurauto reist, is het heel gebruikelijk om onderweg controleposten van de Royal Gendarmerie te passeren.

sections.heading: Embajada del Reino de Marruecos en España Serrano, 179 28002 Madrid Teléfono: 91 563 10 90 Fax: 91 561 78 87​
Sito en 29, Rue D'Alger, se encuentra en obras, únicamente permanece en esta dirección la sección de legalizaciones.

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Higher levels apply in some areas.
Avoid demonstrations, protests, large public events and intercommunal disputes, as they can turn violent.
There are several curfews in place across Ghana due to criminal incidents.
Plan your travel movements to avoid breaking curfews and follow the instructions of local authorities (see 'Safety').
We advise: Exercise a high degree of caution in Ghana overall due to the threat of violent crime.

sections.heading: Local emergency contacts
added: Fire and rescue services Call 192.
Medical emergencies Call 193 or go to the hospital.
Police Call 191 or go to the nearest police station.
Latest update We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution in Ghana overall due to the threat of violent crime.
Higher levels apply in some areas.

sections.heading: Safety
added: There's been an increase in incidents of banditry involving passenger buses travelling in the Upper West and Upper East regions resulting in injuries and deaths.
Intercommunal disputes and violence has increased near the northern borders areas with Côte d’Ivoire and Burkina Faso causing deaths and displacement of communities.
If you're travelling in the area you should keep informed about the situation, seek local advice and take precautions.
If you're travelling to Ghana, you could be a target for violent crime, including assault, sexual assault, kidnapping and armed robbery.
There are increased security risks if you're a woman travelling alone.

sections.heading: Health
added: There's currently an outbreak of mpox in parts of Africa, including Ghana.
Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone who has mpox (see ‘Health risks’).

Yellow fever, malaria and other insect-borne diseases are common, as are waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases, such as cholera, hepatitis, lassa fever, dengue fever, meningococcal meningitis, bilharzia and tuberculosis.
Avoid insect bites, ice cubes, and raw or undercooked food.
Drink boiled or bottled water.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: You're subject to all local laws and penalties, including those that may appear harsh by Australian standards.
Some activities that are legal in Australia are illegal in Ghana.
Penalties for drug offences are severe.
They include mandatory jail terms.
Engaging in same-sex activity is illegal and could lead to prison sentences.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You need a visa to travel to Ghana.
If you're staying for more than 90 days in 12 months, you must register for a National Identification Authority Non-citizen Ghana card.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact the nearest high commission/embassy or consulate of Ghana for the latest details.
You need to show a valid yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Ghana.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian High Commission, Accra.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission’s social media accounts.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Violent crime occurs across Ghana.

This includes: Assault Armed robbery (including in a taxi) Sexual assault Break-ins and burglars may be armed Carjacking The risk of violent incidences can happen at any time of the day but increases after dark and during holiday periods.
Your risk also increases if you're a woman travelling alone.
If you're a victim of a violent crime, including sexual assault, get medical assistance.
HIV/AIDS is common in Ghana.

You can apply for a Syrian EVisa online.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
Dual Australian-Syrian nationals can't enter Lebanon to renew expired Australian passports.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Syria.
Our ability to provide consular services is severely limited.
The Romanian Embassy in Damascus may be able to provide some emergency consular services to Australians in Syria.
For further information, contact the Australian Embassy in Beirut.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy’s social media accounts.

sections.heading: Armed conflict and terrorism
added: We continue to advise do not travel to Syria due to the extremely dangerous security situation and the threat of armed conflict, air strikes, terrorism, arbitrary detention and kidnapping.
We have advised do not travel to Syria since 2011.
If you're in Syria despite this advice, prepare to leave when it is safe to do so.
Armed clashes are ongoing in parts of Syria and have resulted in many civilian casualties.
Additional road closures and checkpoints may be established.

sections.heading: Kidnapping
added: There is a very serious threat of kidnapping throughout Syria.
Kidnappings can occur for political or financial gain.
Criminal and terrorist kidnappings are also a significant risk in Syria.
There have been multiple cases of kidnappings in Syria, including of foreign nationals.
The situation in Syria remains fragile and there are numerous terrorist and militant groups operating throughout the country.

changes.description: Inhoud van het advies gewijzigd (zonder nieuwe brondatum).
changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:03 June 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution in Panama due to the high threat of crime. Pay attention to your personal security in crowded areas (see 'Safety'). Take care of your belongings, especially in transport hubs. You need to complete an online immigration and customs declaration form when you enter Panama (see 'Tra

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Pay attention to your personal security in crowded areas (see 'Safety').
Take care of your belongings, especially in transport hubs.
You need to complete an online immigration and customs declaration form when you enter Panama (see 'Travel').
We advise: Exercise a high degree of caution in Panama overall due to the threat of crime.
Higher levels apply in some areas.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Panama has high crime rates.
Petty crime is more common than violent crime.
Take care of your belongings, especially in transport hubs.
Violent crime includes armed robbery and muggings.
Avoid high-crime areas of Panama City.

sections.heading: Health
added: Yellow fever is widespread.
Get vaccinated before you travel.
Malaria is a year-round risk in Bocas del Toro, Darien and San Blas.
It's not a risk in Panama City.
Consider taking anti-malarial medication.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences are severe.
They include long prison sentences.
Officials may also arrest you if you're with someone who's using drugs.
Always carry photo identification, such as a certified copy of your passport.

sections.heading: Travel
added: If you arrive by air or land, you won't need a visa.
You'll be granted an initial stay of 90 days when you arrive.
You must complete an immigration and customs declaration form when you enter Panama.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Panama for the latest details or check with your airline or travel provider.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Australia has an Honorary Consulate in Panama City.
This consulate provides limited consular assistance to Australians in Panama.

sections.heading: Petty crime
added: Petty crime is common, mainly pickpocketing and bag-snatching.
You're most at risk in Panama City and Colon.

Take extra care of your valuables in: airports bus terminals public transport To prevent petty crime: be alert after dark avoid being on the streets alone pay attention in crowded areas use ATMs in daylight hours withdraw or change money in secure areas like h

sections.heading: Violent crime
added: Violent crime occurs in Panama.
This includes: murder robbery burglary kidnapping carjacking sexual assault Armed robbery and muggings occur in Panama.
Violent crime is less common than petty crime.

Hotspots for crime in Panama City include: Calidonia San Miguelito Rio Bajo El Chorrillo Veracruz Beach Panama Viejo Casco Viejo 24 de diciembre Santa Ana Juan Diaz shopping areas on Avenida Central Armed criminals may target you at Madden Dam, a tourist site

To protect yourself from violent crime: don’t travel after dark don’t go out alone after dark pay attention to your surroundings, especially in isolated and remote areas only use official border crossings leave valuables and your passport in a secure place avo

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:26 May 2026 Latest update:There's been an increase of Tuberculosis cases in Palau. Follow advice from local authorities to reduce your risk of exposure. Visitor visas are available on arrival. A Palau entry form is required and is available online up to 72 hours before arrival. There's no charge for the form. Be alert to scams (see 'Travel'). We advise: Exerci

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Follow advice from local authorities to reduce your risk of exposure.
Visitor visas are available on arrival.
A Palau entry form is required and is available online up to 72 hours before arrival.
There's no charge for the form.
Be alert to scams (see 'Travel').

sections.heading: Local emergency contacts
added: Fire and rescue services Call 911.
Medical emergencies Call 911.
Police Call 911 or contact the police at your nearest police station.
Latest update There's been an increase of Tuberculosis cases in Palau.
Follow advice from local authorities to reduce your risk of exposure.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Palau has a low crime rate, but petty crime does occur.
Palau experiences tropical storms.
These can disrupt services.
Know your hotel or cruise ship's evacuation plan.
Find out where your nearest shelter is.

sections.heading: Health
added: There's been an increase of Tuberculosis cases in Palau.
Follow advice from local authorities to reduce your risk of exposure.
Insect-borne diseases, including dengue, are present in Palau.
Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof.
Hepatitis B is endemic in Palau.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: It's illegal to bring, import, advertise, sell or use e-cigarettes and vapes in Palau.
It's illegal to bring and use sunscreen products that contain chemical ingredients harmful to coral reefs.
Only use 'reef safe' sunscreen products, which are available in stores in Palau.
Follow local alcohol laws.
The legal drinking age is 21.

sections.heading: Travel
added: A visitor visa for up to 30 days is available on arrival.
You'll need an onward or return ticket and at least 6 months validity remaining on your passport.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You'll need to complete an online Palau Entry Form, up to 72 hours prior to arrival.
Be alert to scams, there is no charge for this form.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
To stay up to date with local information follow the Embassy social media accounts, Facebook @AusEmbPalau and Instagram @AusEmbPW.
The Australian Embassy in Palau can provide consular assistance to Australians in Palau.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Petty crime is uncommon but increasing due to drug abuse.
To protect yourself from theft: be aware of your personal safety protect your valuables don't buy or accept counterfeit goods
