changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:22 June 2026 Latest update:Depending on why you're travelling and how long you plan to stay, you may require a visa for travel to the Schengen area. Check the European Entry and Exit (EES) and visa rules before you travel. If you're a dual national of a European country, check whether the EES applies to you and what passport you should enter on. There may be d

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Check the European Entry and Exit (EES) and visa rules before you travel.
If you're a dual national of a European country, check whether the EES applies to you and what passport you should enter on.
There may be delays at border crossings (see ‘Travel’).
We advise: Exercise normal safety precautions in Switzerland.
Download Switzerland Download Europe

sections.heading: Safety
added: Serious crime levels are low, but petty crime is on the rise.
Take care at tourist spots and on transport, including overnight trains.
Watch out for thieves who use distraction techniques.
Keep your belongings close.
Terrorists have targeted European cities, including transport hubs, churches, other houses of worship and places visited by travellers.

sections.heading: Health
added: The level of health care is high.
All foreigners receive the same level of medical care as Swiss residents.
There are no public hospitals in Switzerland.
You may need to guarantee payment in advance and costs can be extremely high.
Contact your travel insurance provider for advice.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry drugs.
If you're convicted of a crime and you're a foreign national, you may be expelled from Switzerland and unable to return for a long time.
Full travel advice: Local laws

sections.heading: Travel
added: Switzerland is a part of the Schengen area which means you can enter Switzerland without a visa in some cases.
In other situations, you'll need a visa.

If you’re travelling to a country in the Schengen area for a short stay (90 days or less) using an Australian passport, you’ll be required to register your biometric details, such as fingerprints and a photo, when you arrive.
You don’t need to take any action before you arrive at the border, and there is no cost for EES registration.
There may be delays at border crossings.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Bern or the Consulate-General in Geneva.
Full travel advice: Local contacts

sections.heading: Crime
added: While Switzerland has low levels of serious crime, violent offences and assaults can occur, especially in the major cities.
Petty crime, bag snatching, and pickpocketing are increasing.
It regularly happens on public transport and in tourist areas.
Thieves may steal your belongings when your vehicle is unattended or stopped in traffic.

Tourists are a target for robbery: on all trains, particularly overnight trains, as they sleep near tourist attractions in major towns, including Bern, Zurich and Geneva at airports and railway stations Crime rates increase in peak tourist seasons around summe

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorism is a threat worldwide.
Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time.
Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places.
If there’s a terrorist incident, leave the area if it’s safe to do so and follow the instructions of local authorities.
More information: Terrorism

sections.heading: Latest update
added: If you plan to visit forests or national parks during this period, you must obtain written confirmation in advance from the relevant National Forest/National Parks authority (see 'Travel').
We advise: Exercise normal safety precautions in North Macedonia.
Download North Macedonia Download Europe

sections.heading: Roadside assistance
added: Latest update There is a ban on unregistered access to forests and national parks from 1 July to 31 August.
If you plan to visit forests or national parks during this period, you must obtain written confirmation in advance from the relevant National Forest/National Parks authority (see 'Travel').
Advice Level summary We advise: Exercise normal safety precautions in North Macedonia.
Always get a police report when you report a crime.
Your insurer should have a 24-hour emergency number.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Politically motivated civil unrest with the potential to turn violent can occur.
Avoid protests and demonstrations.
Terrorism is a threat worldwide.
Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time.
Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places Petty crime occurs in large cities and at airports.

sections.heading: Health
added: Air pollution can be an issue in parts of North Macedonia, including Skopje.
If you have a breathing condition, talk to your doctor before you travel.
Infectious disease risks include hepatitis, measles, brucellosis and trichinosis.
Boil all drinking water or drink bottled water.
Medical facilities are poor.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry drugs.
Penalties include heavy fines and lengthy prison sentences.
It's illegal to photograph military or police personnel, sites and equipment.
Authorities may treat you as a national of North Macedonia if you have Macedonian heritage.
You may have to get a North Macedonia passport.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of North Macedonia for the latest details.
You can stay for up to 90 days in a 6-month period without a visa.
Local authorities have introduced a complete ban on unregistered access to forests and national parks from 1 July to 31 August.
If you plan to visit forests or national parks during this period, you must obtain written confirmation in advance from the relevant regional National Forest/National Parks authority.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
The Consulate in Skopje can provide limited consular assistance but can't issue passports or provide notarial services.
Get full consular assistance from the Australian Embassy in Belgrade.

sections.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
added: Public protests and events that draw large groups of people can turn violent.
Violent protests have occurred in Skopje and other major cities.
Inter-ethnic violence could happen anywhere.
Security in areas bordering Kosovo is volatile.
Tensions exist between ethnic Macedonian and ethnic Albanian communities in the region.

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:09 June 2026 Latest update:Argentina has raised its national security level following events in the Middle East. There may be increased security in public places, including places of worship and Jewish institutions. There's also increased controls at borders and entry points. Hantavirus continues to be a risk in Argentina, including in and around Buenos Aires

sections.heading: Latest update
added: There may be increased security in public places, including places of worship and Jewish institutions.
There's also increased controls at borders and entry points.
Hantavirus continues to be a risk in Argentina, including in and around Buenos Aires and northern Patagonia.
It's generally spread through contact with infected rodents.
Avoid contact with live or dead rodents, nests, burrows and droppings (see 'Safety').

sections.heading: Local emergency contacts
added: Fire and rescue services Call 100.
Call 911 (English language services only available in the city of Buenos Aires).
Medical emergencies Call 107.
Police Call +54 9 11 5050 9260, for multilingual tourist police (only in the city of Buenos Aires).
Latest update Argentina has raised its national security level following events in the Middle East.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Argentina has raised its national security level following events in the Middle East.
There may be increased security in public places, including places of worship and Jewish institutions.
There's also increased controls at borders and entry points.
Petty crime, including robbery and bag snatching is common.
Take care on public transport and in tourist areas or crowded places.

sections.heading: Health
added: Insect-borne diseases are common in the north of Argentina and as far south as Buenos Aires.
These include dengue, chikungunya and zika virus.
If you're pregnant, discuss your travel plans with your doctor before you leave.
Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof.
Hantavirus continues to be a risk, including in and around Buenos Aires and northern Patagonia.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences are severe and include lengthy prison sentences.
Dual nationals staying in Argentina for more than 180 days must leave Argentina on an Argentine passport.
Make sure you have one before you travel.
The Australian embassy can't help you get one.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Argentina for the latest details.
You can stay as a tourist for up to 90 days without a visa, but you'll need a return or onward ticket, a valid passport and be able to explain the purpose of your trip.
Children travelling alone or with only one parent or legal guardian may require a travel authorisation from the non-accompanying parent(s) or legal guardian(s) to leave the country.
When travelling with both parents, travellers must be able to prove they are the parents or legal guardians (birth certificate or custody documents).

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
To stay up to date with local information follow the Embassy social media accounts, Facebook, Instagram or X Full travel advice: Local contacts

sections.heading: Crime
added: Crime rates are significantly higher than in Australia.
Thefts and robberies are common, especially in Buenos Aires city and its metropolitan area, as well as in other major cities, such as Rosario, Cordoba and Mendoza.
You should keep cash and expensive items such as mobile phones, wallets and jewellery out of sight in public areas.
Avoid lower socioeconomic urban areas where crime is more likely to occur.
Avoid empty streets, especially after dark.

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:02 July 2026 Latest update:Mexico is co-hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, taking place between 11 June and 19 July. If you’re planning to travel to Mexico for the event, see our advice on the FIFA World Cup 2026 for more information (see 'Travel'). Football-related celebrations in Mexico city, have resulted in deaths. Be aware of your surroundings and know wher

sections.heading: Latest update
added: If you’re planning to travel to Mexico for the event, see our advice on the FIFA World Cup 2026 for more information (see 'Travel').
Football-related celebrations in Mexico city, have resulted in deaths.
Be aware of your surroundings and know where the exit points are in case of an emergency or crowd crush.
There have been increased reports of mobile phone theft and drink spiking during the tournament.
We advise exercise a high degree of caution in Mexico overall due to the threat of violent crime.

sections.heading: Safety
added: We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution for the cities of Guadalajara and Puerto Vallarta and we advise exercise a high degree of caution for the other areas in the state of Jalisco.
Mexico has a high risk of violent crime, including murder, armed robbery, sexual assault, and kidnapping.
Carjackers and armed robbers may target people travelling at traffic lights.
Don't travel at night outside major cities.
Drug-related violence is widespread.

sections.heading: Health
added: Malaria and Zika virus are risks.
If you're pregnant, ask your doctor about the risk of Zika virus before you travel.
Mexico has insect-borne diseases, including dengue fever, chikungunya, Chagas disease and leishmaniasis.
Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof.
Mexico has experienced a measles outbreak.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Smoking, including vaping, is banned in all public places, including beaches, parks, hotels and restaurants.
Importing electronic cigarettes and vaping devices is also prohibited.
You may be fined or arrested.
Some activities are illegal for foreigners.
These include political activity, driving without insurance, and failing to report a road accident.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Mexico is co-hosting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, taking place between 11 June and 19 July.
If you’re planning to travel to Mexico for the event, see our advice on the FIFA World Cup 2026 for more information.
If you have an ordinary passport with a chip, and you're visiting for tourism, you can use the e-gates available at a number of major airports.
You'll receive a printed QR code where you can check the number of days you're authorised to stay in Mexico.
If you don't have an ordinary passport with a chip, and you're visiting for 180 days or less as a tourist, you'll receive a visa on arrival for the duration of your planned stay.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy's social media accounts: Facebook, X, Instagram.
The Australian Embassy in Mexico City can provide consular assistance by email, phone, or appointment.

sections.heading: Crime
added: We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution for the cities of Guadalajara and Puerto Vallarta and we now advise exercise a high degree of caution for the other areas in the state of Jalisco.
Stay alert and follow the advice of local authorities.
Security incidents have also been reported in other parts of Mexico, including Tamaulipas, Michoacan, Guerrero, and Nuevo Leon.

sections.heading: Petty crime
added: Petty crime is common, mainly pickpocketing and bag-snatching.
You’re most at risk in big cities.

Take extra care of your valuables in: airports bus terminals public transport To prevent petty crime: be alert after dark avoid being on the streets alone pay attention in crowded areas use ATMs in daylight hours withdraw or change money in secure areas like h
More information: Preventing crime and petty theft

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:14 April 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise exercise normal safety precautions overall. Depending on why you're travelling and how long you plan to stay, you may require a visa for travel to the Schengen area. Check the European Entry and Exit (EES) and visa rules before you travel. If you're a dual national of a European country, check whether the EES a

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Depending on why you're travelling and how long you plan to stay, you may require a visa for travel to the Schengen area.
Check the European Entry and Exit (EES) and visa rules before you travel.
If you're a dual national of a European country, check whether the EES applies to you and what passport you should enter on.
There may be delays at border crossings (see ‘Travel’).
There's an ongoing threat of terrorism in Germany.

sections.heading: Overview
added: The situation in the Middle East remains unpredictable.
Military conflict in the Middle East region may cause travel disruptions and your travel plans may be affected even if your destination is not the Middle East.
‘Reconsider your need to travel’ also means ‘reconsider your need to transit’.
If you need to transit any level 3 locations, stay as short a time as possible and eliminate unnecessary activities.
In Europe, prioritise your safety, monitor developments closely and follow local authorities’ advice, including any warnings to avoid areas deemed to be at high risk of targeting.

sections.heading: Safety
added: There's an ongoing threat of terrorism.
Terrorists have staged attacks and threatened more.
They may target transport hubs and places foreigners visit, including Christmas markets.
Attacks could occur anywhere at any time.
There have been multiple attacks using vehicles or knives in recent years.

sections.heading: Health
added: Take care in forests and parks, including urban areas, as there is a risk of tick‑borne encephalitis.
Ticks are especially active from March to November.
Check for ticks and remove them as soon as possible.
Medical care and facilities are of a high standard.
If you don't have insurance, hospitals may ask for up-front payment.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Nazi symbols, salutes, songs or material, such as flags or memorabilia, are all illegal.
Full travel advice: Local laws

sections.heading: Travel
added: Germany is a part of the Schengen area which means you can enter Germany without a visa in some cases.
In other situations, you'll need a visa.

If you’re travelling to a country in the Schengen area for a short stay (90 days or less) using an Australian passport, you’ll be required to register your biometric details, such as fingerprints and a photo, when you arrive.
You don’t need to take any action before you arrive at the border, and there is no cost for EES registration.
There may be delays at border crossings.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Berlin or Consulate-General in Frankfurt.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the embassy’s social media accounts.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorism is a threat worldwide.
Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time.
Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places.
Germany maintains strong security measures, including at airports and major train stations and authorities continue to disrupt plots and arrest suspected terrorists.
Germany has experienced several attacks in recent years, including knife and vehicle assaults.

changes.description: Inhoud van het advies gewijzigd (zonder nieuwe brondatum).
changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:06 June 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise reconsider your need to travel to Nigeria due to the threat of terrorism, kidnapping, violent crime and the risk of civil unrest. Higher levels apply in some areas. We now advise do not travel to Kwara State, in addition to a number of other states in Nigeria. There's a high risk of terrorist attacks across Nige

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Higher levels apply in some areas.
We now advise do not travel to Kwara State, in addition to a number of other states in Nigeria.
There's a high risk of terrorist attacks across Nigeria.
Terrorist attacks may be more likely around religious holidays and other significant dates.
Always be alert to threats, especially in public places.

sections.heading: Safety
added: There's a high risk of terrorist attacks across Nigeria by various militant groups.
Attacks could be indiscriminate or may target foreign interests.
Terrorist attacks may be more likely around religious holidays and other significant dates.

Potential targets include places where crowds gather, such as government buildings, law enforcement and military facilities, hotels, bars, restaurants, political meetings, places of worship, schools, markets, shopping malls, sporting events, transport hubs and
Avoid all demonstrations, strikes, rallies and large gatherings.

sections.heading: Health
added: Other insect-borne diseases include malaria and dengue.
Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof.
Consider taking anti-malarial medication.
Check your vaccinations are up to date before you travel.
Poliovirus outbreaks have occurred in Nigeria in the last 12 months (Circulating Vaccine Derived Poliovirus Type 2).

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences include long prison sentences and large fines.
Serious crimes, such as murder and armed robbery, can attract the death penalty.
Nigeria has strict laws against same-sex relationships.
Avoid public displays of affection and adhere to conservative dress codes.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You'll need a visa to travel to Nigeria.
You can apply and pay for a visa online on the Nigeria Immigration Service website.
Complete landing and departure cards online before entering or exiting the country.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
Contact the nearest embassy, high commission or consulate of Nigeria for details about visas, customs and quarantine rules.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission’s social media accounts.
The Australian High Commission in Abuja and Australia’s Honorary Consulate in Lagos provide limited consular and passport services.
If you travel to 'do not travel' areas despite our advice, we may not be able to provide you with consular services.

sections.heading: Security situation
added: We continue to advise reconsider your need to travel due to the threat of terrorism, kidnapping, violent crime and the risk of civil unrest.
Higher levels apply in some areas.
We also advise do not travel to Kwara State, in addition to a number of other 'do not travel' states in Nigeria.
If you travel to Nigeria, you're at risk of: terrorist attack kidnapping civil unrest crime The security situation could change suddenly.
You could get caught up in violence directed at others.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: There's a high risk of terrorist attacks in Nigeria by various militant groups.
Further attacks are highly likely.
If you're in a 'do not travel' area, leave if it's safe to do so.

Terrorist attacks: can happen anywhere and at any time may specifically target religious and public holidays may be indiscriminate may target venues frequently visited by foreign nationals Potential targets may include: hotels, bars, restaurants, markets and s
Australian officials rarely travel outside of Abuja and Lagos, due to the high-risk security environment.

changes.description: Inhoud van het advies gewijzigd (zonder nieuwe brondatum).
changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:16 January 2026 Latest update:Public protests and events that draw large groups of people can turn violent. Stay away from crowds, check media updates and follow the instructions of local authorities (see 'Safety'). You don't need a visa for tourist visits of up to 90 days in a 6-month period. You'll need a visa for other situations. Entry and exit conditions

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Stay away from crowds, check media updates and follow the instructions of local authorities (see 'Safety').
You don't need a visa for tourist visits of up to 90 days in a 6-month period.
You'll need a visa for other situations.
Entry and exit conditions can change with little notice (see 'Travel').
We advise: Exercise normal safety precautions in Serbia.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Protests are common in Belgrade and other towns and cities.
They can disrupt transport and other essential services.
Avoid protests and large gatherings as many have been violent.
Foreigners have been detained after attending protests.
Monitor local media for updates and follow the advice of local authorities.

sections.heading: Health
added: Air pollution can be high in some Serbian cities, including Belgrade.
If you have a breathing condition, talk to your doctor before you travel.
Medical facilities are below Australian standards.
If you become seriously ill or injured, you may need to be evacuated.
Make sure your insurance covers this.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
It's illegal to photograph military and police personnel and equipment.
Serbia recognises dual nationality.
As a dual citizen, you may have to complete outstanding military service obligations.
Check with an embassy or consulate of Serbia.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Serbia for the latest details.
You don't need a visa for tourist visits of up to 90 days in a 6-month period.
This applies from the date of the first entry.
You'll need a visa for other situations.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Belgrade.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the embassy’s social media accounts.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorism is a threat worldwide.
Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time.
Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places.
In June 2024, one person was killed in an attack outside of the Israeli embassy in Belgrade.

Foreigners have been detained after attending protests.
Some sporting events have turned violent, injuring people and damaging property.
There have been clashes between police and fans near sports venues and between fans of opposing teams.

Pay attention to your belongings when leaving cruise ships or transferring to buses and local transport.

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Both can be completed on the All Indonesia website.
Make sure you understand your visa conditions, entry and exit requirements.
Ensure you have the right visa for your trip.
It's illegal to work, conduct research or volunteer if you're on a tourist visa.
This includes creating or posting online content for payment or commercial purposes (see ‘Travel’).

sections.heading: Safety
added: There's an ongoing risk of terrorist attack.
Popular tourist areas and government buildings may be the target of terrorist attacks.
Be alert to possible threats.
Take official warnings seriously and follow the advice of local authorities.
There are regular instances of violence in the provinces of Papua Pegunungan (Highland Papua) and Papua Tengah (Central Papua), as well as towns close to the border of these provinces.

sections.heading: Health
added: Before you travel to Indonesia, you must complete a health declaration online as part of the new All Indonesia Arrival Card and within 72 hours (3 days) of your travel to Indonesia.
Beware of fake websites charging a fee to submit the arrival card.
Only use official government sites, which will include ‘go.id’ in the website's address.
The standard of medical facilities is generally lower than in Australia.
Many regional hospitals only provide basic facilities.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Indonesia's revised criminal code includes penalties for cohabitation, adultery and sex outside of marriage between a man and a woman.
If you're married, authorities can act on a complaint from your spouse.
If you're unmarried, authorities can act on a complaint from your child or parent.
The death penalty exists for some crimes, including drug offences.
Penalties for ordering, possessing or using illegal drugs can include heavy fines, long prison sentences and the death penalty.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Before arriving in Indonesia (by plane or boat), you'll need to complete the All Indonesia Arrival Card online and within 72 hours (3 days) of your travel.
You can fill out the arrival card and then apply for an e-Visa on Arrival (e-VOA) on the All Indonesia website or apply for an e-VOA separately at The Official eVisa website for Indonesia.
Beware of fake websites charging a fee to submit the arrival card.
Only use official government sites, which will include ‘go.id’ in the website's address.
Always carry photo ID (or a copy).

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy, Jakarta, the Australian Consulate-General, Bali, the Australian Consulate-General, Makassar or the Australian Consulate-General, Surabaya.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy's social media accounts.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: The terrorist threat in Indonesia is ongoing.
Attacks could happen anywhere and anytime, including at popular tourist areas and government buildings.
Terrorists in Indonesia may carry out small-scale violent attacks with little or no warning.
Take official warnings seriously and follow the advice of local authorities.
Remain respectful of religious and local customs.

sections.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
added: Usually, protests are planned in advance, but can occur with little or no notice.

Protests and events are often held near important government buildings and embassies, including the Australian Embassy in Jakarta, Australian Consulates-General in Surabaya, Bali and Makassar, or the offices of international organisations.
Phone or email ahead for an appointment before going to the Embassy or the Consulates-General (see Local contacts).
Protests can cause traffic delays and make it hard to reach some locations.
Demonstrations and acts of violence can happen when courts try and sentence extremists.

Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time.
Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places.
There's an ongoing threat of terrorism in Austria.
There have been terrorist attacks and arrests related to planned attacks.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Austria has a low rate of serious crime.

Petty crime is common on public transport and in tourist areas, including: bag snatching pickpocketing Hotspots for crime include: Vienna's largest train stations — Hauptbahnhof, Westbahnhof, Meidling and Praterstern.

International trains and buses Tourist attractions, including St Stephen's Cathedral, the Imperial Palace (the Hofburg) and shopping areas To protect yourself from crime: watch your belongings in crowded areas and on trains, buses and taxis do not place valuab

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:07 April 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise do not travel to Myanmar. The situation in Myanmar remains unpredictable. The security situation, including in major cities such as Yangon, may become unstable at short notice and authorities can introduce travel restrictions at any time. Attacks may be planned against locations that foreigners frequent, includ

sections.heading: Latest update
added: The situation in Myanmar remains unpredictable.
The security situation, including in major cities such as Yangon, may become unstable at short notice and authorities can introduce travel restrictions at any time.
Attacks may be planned against locations that foreigners frequent, including public spaces and civilian infrastructure.
Attacks are unpredictable in their location and intensity.
Consider your travel plans carefully.

sections.heading: Safety
added: The situation in Myanmar remains unpredictable.
Explosions and other security incidents occur in Yangon and frequently in other parts of the country.
Attacks are unpredictable in their location and intensity.

Attacks may be planned against locations frequented by foreigners, including public spaces and civilian infrastructure, such as hotels and serviced apartments, restaurants, bars, shopping malls, schools, petrol stations and supermarkets.
Minimise movement, especially on anniversaries and national days of significance.

sections.heading: Health
added: Medical facilities are severely stretched by the security situation, particularly outside Yangon.
Hospitals have closed or ceased providing services at short notice.
Medical evacuations may be delayed or not possible.
Talk to your travel or insurance provider.
Be particularly cautious if you have an existing medical condition.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Myanmar authorities often don't tell Embassy staff about Australians they've arrested, detained or deported.
If you're arrested or jailed ask to contact the Australian Embassy immediately.
A conscription law came into effect in 2024.
If local authorities consider you to be a citizen of Myanmar, you may be required to serve in the armed forces.
The length of service can depend on criteria such as age, occupation, and whether there is a state of emergency.

sections.heading: Travel
added: If you decide to travel to Myanmar despite our advice, there are international flights to and from Yangon and Mandalay.
Book directly with airlines or through your travel agent.
You must stay in registered hotels and guesthouses.
Register your address with immigration authorities if you're staying more than 90 days.
Inform the authorities if you change hotels or will be away from your address.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Yangon.
Our ability to provide consular services in Myanmar may be limited.
Full travel advice: Local contacts

sections.heading: Security situation
added: The Myanmar military assumed control of the country and declared a state of emergency on 1 February 2021.
There has been a significant increase in violence, with many deaths, injuries and arrests.
There have been widespread detentions, including of foreigners.
Australians may be at risk of arbitrary detention.
Do not take photos of protests, and military personnel or sites.

sections.heading: Armed conflict
added: There are active conflicts involving armed groups and security forces across Myanmar.
Explosions and other security incidents targeting security forces occur within Yangon and in other parts of the country.
Anti-regime attacks, carried out by a range of actors, have targeted government buildings, including military-affiliated regime schools, hospitals, hotels, shopping centres and public transport.
Attacks have resulted in civilian and military casualties and are unpredictable in their location and intensity.
Attacks may be planned against locations that are also frequented by foreigners such as hotels, apartments, restaurants and public spaces.

sections.heading: Latest update
added: We advise: Exercise a high degree of caution in Jamaica due to the threat of violent crime.
Download Jamaica Download Americas

sections.heading: Local emergency contacts
added: Fire and rescue services Call 119.
Medical emergencies Call 119, or go to the hospital.
Latest update If you've been in an Ebola-affected country in the last 21 days, you will be subject to enhanced health screening on arrival in Jamaica.
Advice Level summary We advise: Exercise a high degree of caution in Jamaica due to the threat of violent crime.

sections.heading: Safety
added: There has been a recent reduction in crime rates, however violent crime is a serious problem.
Crimes include gang-related armed robbery and murder.
Avoid walking alone or on beaches at night.
If you're attacked or robbed, don't resist.
Criminals are often armed.

sections.heading: Health
added: HIV/AIDS is widespread in Jamaica.
Take precautions if you're engaging in high-risk activities.
Insect-borne illnesses, especially dengue, are common.
Check your accommodation is insect-proof.
Cases of zika virus have been reported.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences are severe.
You can be jailed for having illegal drugs.
You'll be screened for drugs when leaving Jamaica.
Jamaica has decriminalised the possession of up to 2 ounces of marijuana.

sections.heading: Travel
added: If you've been in an Ebola-affected country in the last 21 days, you will be subject to enhanced health screening on arrival in Jamaica.
You don't need a visa to enter Jamaica if your visit is for tourism and you're staying for less than 6 months.
You don't need a visa for business purposes if you're going to stay for less than 30 days.
You need to complete the Immigration/Customs C5 Form online before arriving in Jamaica.
(See 'Travel') Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Australia has a consulate in Kingston, headed by an honorary consul.
It provides limited consular services and conducts passport interviews.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Violent gang and drug-related crimes are common.

This includes: murder kidnapping shootings armed robbery sexual assault carjacking home invasions Hotspots for violent crime include parts of: Kingston St Catherine Montego Bay May Pen Criminals have targeted travellers after accepting spiked food or drink, in
Travellers have reported assaults carried out by resort staff and other travellers.
Most hotels and resorts are well-guarded but always lock your doors and windows.

To protect yourself from crime: always be alert to your surroundings stay somewhere safe with good security only use ATMs in hotels, shopping centres or other controlled areas during the day be aware of who's around you when using ATMs arrange transport to/fro

Be vigilant in public places and follow the advice of local authorities (see 'Safety'). We advise: Exercise norma

sections.heading: Latest update
added: You must complete a Malaysia Digital Arrival Card (MDAC) before you arrive in Malaysia (see 'Travel').
There's a risk of terrorism in Malaysia.
Be vigilant in public places and follow the advice of local authorities (see 'Safety').
We advise: Exercise normal safety precautions in Malaysia overall.
Higher levels apply in some areas.

sections.heading: Safety
added: There is a risk of terrorism in Malaysia.
Attacks could be random and small-scale.
Possible targets include tourist areas and other locations frequented by foreigners, including in Kuala Lumpur (see Full Advice - Safety below).
Take official warnings seriously.
There's an ongoing risk of kidnapping in offshore and coastal areas of Eastern Sabah.

sections.heading: Health
added: Strict rules control the importation of medication.
If you plan to bring medication, check if it's legal in Malaysia.
Dengue fever is widespread, including in major cities.
Zika virus is also a risk.
Malaria is a risk in rural areas.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: If you're suspected of using drugs, you may be required to take a urine test on arrival.
This includes if you're travelling from a country where possession and use of drugs such as cannabis is legal.
Penalties for drug offences are severe.
Don't use, carry or traffic illegal drugs.
Punishments include the death penalty.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You must complete a Malaysia Digital Arrival Card (MDAC) before you arrive.
The MDAC must be submitted through the Malaysian Immigration website.
See the Malaysian Immigration website for further information, including exemption details.
In most circumstances, you can get a 90-day tourist visa on arrival.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian High Commission in Kuala Lumpur.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission's social media accounts.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorist attacks could happen in Malaysia.
Attacks could be random and may affect locations popular with foreigners, or during major events or holidays that attract large crowds.
Malaysian authorities have arrested people for planning terror attacks.
This includes attacks at venues in Kuala Lumpur.

Other possible targets include: hotels, clubs, restaurants and cafes schools LGBTQIA+ venues and events markets and shopping malls places of worship or religious holidays outdoor recreation events tourist areas government facilities embassies public transport

sections.heading: Kidnapping
added: Kidnapping can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe.
Foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped overseas while travelling.
Terrorists and criminal groups pose an ongoing threat of kidnapping in the coastal areas of Eastern Sabah, including islands, on boat and ferry services, dive sites and other tourist facilities.
Extremists based in the southern Philippines may be active in the area between the towns of Terusan and Tawau in Eastern Sabah, including offshore islands such as Lankayan Island.
The risk increases after nightfall.

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Protect yourself against mosquito bites and use insect repellent (see 'Health').
We advise: Exercise normal safety precautions in Vanuatu.
Download Vanuatu Download Pacific

sections.heading: Medical emergencies
added: Call 115 for the Pro-Medical Ambulance Service (Port Vila and Lungaville only) or go to a hospital.
Call the ProMedical Ambulance Service on 115 (Port Vila and Luganville only) or go to a hospital.

sections.heading: Safety
added: The cyclone season is from November to April, but cyclones and tropical storms can sometimes occur outside this.
Cyclones can disrupt services and cause property damage, injuries, and loss of life.
In the event of a cyclone, check the National Disaster Management Office and Vanuatu Meteorological and Geo-Hazards Department websites.
Earthquakes occur around Vanuatu.
A 7.2-magnitude earthquake struck Port Vila in December 2024, causing damage to infrastructure, including roads and buildings.

sections.heading: Health
added: A Dengue Outbreak was officially declared in June in the south of Efate Island, Shefa Province.
Protect yourself against mosquito bites and use insect repellent.
In September 2025, pertussis (whooping cough) outbreaks were declared on Tanna, Efate, Erromango, Nguna and Futuna Islands.
If your last pertussis-containing vaccine was more than 10 years ago, or five-years ago for high-risk travellers, a booster dose is recommended.
Malaria is present especially in the north of the country, in the Sanma, Penama and Torba provinces.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
If you're convicted of a drug offence, you could face fines or jail time.
There are strict rules regarding obscene material.
Penalties include prison sentences.
Be aware of laws around alcohol.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You can apply for a tourist visa on arrival in Vanuatu if you are visiting for tourism and leisure for up to 120 days.
To qualify, you must have a booking at an accredited tourism facility or carry a letter of support from family or friends.
You should hold a return ticket, and your passport must be valid for at least six months from the date of entry into Vanuatu.
Full travel advice: Travel

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Contact the Australian High Commission in Port Vila for consular assistance.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission’s social media accounts.

sections.heading: Extreme weather and natural disasters
added: A 7.2-magnitude earthquake struck Port Vila in December 2024, causing damage to infrastructure, including roads and buildings.
Many hotels and tourism operators and businesses are open for business, but some are yet to reopen or are operating from temporary locations.

The cyclone season is from November to April.
Monitor weather updates and warnings (see 'Extreme weather and natural disasters').
You need a visa to enter Madagascar.

sections.heading: Safety
added: The cyclone season is from November to April, but extreme weather events can occur at other times.
These can cause heavy rain, flooding, strong winds and landslides.
Check weather conditions when planning your travel and monitor local media for weather forecasts and warnings (see 'Extreme weather and natural disasters' below).
Civil unrest is common in Madagascar and protests can turn violent.
Security forces have been known to respond with force.

sections.heading: Health
added: Medical facilities in Madagascar are basic.
You'll need to be evacuated if you become seriously ill or injured.
All hospitals require up front payments, and private clinics can be expensive.
Check your insurance covers hospital stays and evacuation.
There is a high exposure risk to diseases.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties are severe and may include long prison sentences.
Know and follow local laws.
Always carry photo identification or a copy of your passport or ID.
Police may ask for it, especially if you're out late at night.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You'll need a visa to enter Madagascar.
If you're a tourist, this can be completed on arrival at the airport for visits up to 60 days.
Check with local authorities for the latest fees.
Make sure your passport is stamped when passing through immigration.
Take care not to overstay your visa otherwise you may be detained or deported.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.

sections.heading: Crime

Petty crimes, such as bag-snatching and pickpocketing are common, particularly in urban and crowded areas such as markets.
Foreigners have also been the target of muggings and theft.

To protect yourself: be vigilant in tourist and transport locations don't go out alone after dark especially on foot don't leave luggage and valuables unattended don't display cash or valuables always keep your vehicle and accommodation locked If you're attack

sections.heading: Latest update

You must hold a valid visa or Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) card to enter India.
You're required to complete the online e-Arrival Card up to 3 days before travel to India.
This applies to Australian citizens and OCI card holders.
Depending on your travel route, you might be asked to complete a paper arrival card (see 'Travel').

sections.heading: Safety
added: Terrorism remains a risk.
Terrorist activities, violent demonstrations and armed clashes sometimes occur.
Avoid crowded places and possible targets.
Large crowds at religious ceremonies, major sport events and other gatherings can also be dangerous.
People have been killed in stampedes.

sections.heading: Health
added: Medical facilities in major cities have adequate treatment standards.
The availability of treatment can be very limited or unavailable in remote and rural areas.
You may need to be evacuated if you're seriously ill or injured.
Medical evacuation can be very expensive.
Check you have travel insurance.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Always carry your passport and ensure you have a valid visa.
Carrying or using a satellite phone or satellite-enabled GPS devices (including emergency locator beacons) without official permission is illegal.
You could be arrested, detained, receive a large fine, or have the device taken from you.
Your travel could be significantly delayed.
Don't use or carry illegal drugs.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You must apply for a visa before arrival.
Check your eligibility and apply online for an e-visa by visiting the Government of India's Indian Visa Online website.

For information on other visa types and immigration requirements, see the Foreigners Registration Office (FRRO) and Bureau of Indian Immigration websites or contact the nearest high commission/embassy or consulate of India for the latest details.
You're now required to complete an online health self-declaration form up to 24 hours before arrival in India, due to the Ebola disease outbreak overseas.
If you don't complete the form before you arrive, expect delays and additional screening procedures at Indian immigration.

sections.heading: Local contacts

sections.heading: Terrorism

The Indian Government regularly issues public alert warnings about possible terrorist attacks.
Terrorist attacks could target foreigners.
Violence directed at others may affect you.

sections.heading: Jammu and Kashmir

Check local media and follow the advice of local authorities.
In the Union Territory of Jammu and Kashmir there is a high risk of: terrorist attacks violent demonstrations There is a higher risk in rural areas and areas close to the border with Pakistan.
Terrorists have kidnapped foreigners in Jammu and Kashmir.
Terrorists have also targeted tourist buses.

sections.heading: Latest update
added: You're subject to all local laws and penalties, which can be interpreted broadly and applied harshly by Australian standards.
Australians may be at risk of arbitrary detention or enforcement of local laws for unintentionally breaking the law (see 'Local laws').
There is an ongoing and high threat of terrorism and kidnapping throughout Burkina Faso.
The security situation in Burkina Faso continues to be volatile.
Terrorist attacks can occur throughout Burkina Faso, including in Ouagadougou.

sections.heading: Safety
added: We continue to advise do not travel to Burkina Faso.
The Australian High Commission in Accra has very limited capacity to provide consular assistance in Burkina Faso.
If, despite our advice, you travel to Burkina Faso, you should get professional security advice.
There's an ongoing high threat of terrorism and kidnapping.
Extremist militant groups continue to attack targets across Burkina Faso.

sections.heading: Health
added: Yellow fever, malaria and other insect-borne diseases are common.
Make sure your accommodation is insect-proof.
Take precautions if you're engaging in high-risk activities to avoid the risk of sexually transmissible infections (STIs) and HIV.
Medical facilities are poor, and emergency services are very limited.
You may need to pay cash up-front, even in an emergency.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: You're subject to all laws and penalties, which can be interpreted broadly and applied harshly by Australian standards.
You could unintentionally break the law and be at risk of arbitrary detention or harsh enforcement of local laws.
It's illegal to take photos of military zones, assets or personnel.
It's also illegal to take photos of airports, government buildings (including foreign diplomatic missions) and places of worship and religious monuments.
It's illegal to export art or cultural objects without government approval, including old or traditional artworks and all old material of national cultural significance.

sections.heading: Travel

Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
The security situation in Burkina Faso remains volatile.
A state of emergency exists in some regions and provinces.
Some locations are also subject to curfew.

sections.heading: Local Contacts
added: There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
The Australian High Commission in Accra has very limited capacity to provide consular assistance in West Africa.
Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Burkina Faso.
For consular assistance, contact the Canadian Embassy in Ouagadougou by phone or email.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: The security situation across Burkina Faso is dangerous and volatile, and there's an ongoing and very high threat of terrorist attacks and kidnapping.
Kidnapping by terrorist groups is a very high risk across Burkina Faso, and is particularly high in areas bordering Mali and Niger.
Several foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped in Burkina Faso.
Active terrorist groups maintain the intent and capability to kidnap foreigners.
Terrorists and other armed groups regularly conduct attacks targeting security forces, civilians and foreign interests.

sections.heading: Kidnapping
added: Kidnapping occurs across the world with political, ideological, and criminal motives.
Foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped overseas whilst travelling.
Kidnaps can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe.
Kidnapping by terrorist groups is a very high risk across Burkina Faso, and is particularly high in areas bordering Mali and Niger.
Several foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped in Burkina Faso.

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:07 July 2026 Latest update:Depending on why you're travelling and how long you plan to stay, you may require a visa for travel to the Schengen area. Check the European Entry and Exit (EES) and visa rules before you travel. If you're a dual national of a European country, check whether the EES applies to you and what passport you should enter on. There may be d

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Check the European Entry and Exit (EES) and visa rules before you travel.
If you're a dual national of a European country, check whether the EES applies to you and what passport you should enter on.
There may be delays at border crossings (see ‘Travel’).
Be aware of pickpockets and bag snatchers at tourist spots and on transport, particularly at central train stations.
Thieves often operate in groups and use distraction techniques, such as staging street performances, to execute their plans.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Italy experiences earthquakes and volcanic activity.
Large earthquakes cause landslides and avalanches.
Forest fires are common from June to September.
Monitor the media and follow the advice of local authorities.
Visit the Italian Civil Protection Authority website or follow them on social media for more information.

sections.heading: Health
added: If you need urgent medical assistance, call 112.
English-speaking operators are available.
Medical facilities are good in major cities and limited in rural areas.
Australia has a Reciprocal Health Care Agreement with Italy.
You can get emergency treatment in public medical facilities if you show your Medicare card and Australian passport.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Authorities may request to see your ID, including your passport, at any time.
Ensure you’re carrying valid identification.
You may be fined for littering, sitting, eating or drinking near churches and public buildings or in public spaces.
Obey signs about conduct.
Organised pub crawls are banned in some cities, including Rome.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Italy is a part of the Schengen area which means you can enter Italy without a visa in some cases.
In other situations, you'll need a visa.

If you’re travelling to a country in the Schengen area for a short stay (90 days or less) using an Australian passport, you’ll be required to register your biometric details, such as fingerprints and a photo, when you arrive.
You don’t need to take any action before you arrive at the border, and there is no cost for EES registration.
There may be delays at border crossings.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Rome or the Consulate-General in Milan.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the embassy's social media accounts.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Petty crime is common, particularly in the summer and autumn tourist seasons.
It includes bag snatching, pick-pocketing, passport theft and theft from cars.

Thieves are most active: in larger cities in and around major tourist spots churches and places of worship in parked cars on public transport at major airports at railway stations and bus terminals at beaches (including cars parked near beaches) at hotel recep

sections.heading: Theft from cars
added: Vehicle break-in and theft is common.
Many Australians have had belongings, including passports and other valuables stolen from their parked cars.
Avoid leaving luggage in your car for any length of time.
Large tour buses and campervans on busy streets are routinely targeted.
There are reports of thieves slashing tyres and staging roadside emergencies.

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:23 June 2026 Latest update:We’ve reviewed our advice for Israel and have lowered the overall travel advice level. We now advise reconsider your need to travel due to the unpredictable security situation in Israel and the region. We continue to advise do not travel to border areas with Gaza (Palestine), Lebanon and Syria. Follow warnings from local authorities

sections.heading: Latest update
added: We now advise reconsider your need to travel due to the unpredictable security situation in Israel and the region.
We continue to advise do not travel to border areas with Gaza (Palestine), Lebanon and Syria.
Follow warnings from local authorities to avoid areas deemed to be at high risk of targeting.
The local security situation could deteriorate with little or no notice.
Flight operations may be suspended due to airspace closures and flight schedules may change at short notice.

sections.heading: Middle East conflict
added: The security situation in the Middle East region remains unpredictable.
Military conflict in the region may cause travel disruptions and your travel plans may be affected.
‘Reconsider your need to travel’ also means ‘reconsider your need to transit’.
If you need to travel to any level 3 locations, stay as short a time as possible and eliminate unnecessary activities.
Advice levels can go up at short notice if conditions deteriorate.

sections.heading: Safety
added: We’ve reviewed our advice for Israel and have lowered the overall travel advice level.
We now advise reconsider your need to travel due to the unpredictable security situation in Israel and the region.
Follow any warnings from local authorities to avoid areas deemed to be at high risk of targeting.
Flight operations may be suspended due to airspace closures and flight schedules may change at short notice.
Check the status of any border crossing before you travel by air, land or sea.

sections.heading: Health
added: Infectious diseases are a risk.
Drink boiled or bottled water.
West Nile virus can occur, and there is no vaccine to prevent it.
Use insect repellent and make sure your accommodation is insect-proof.
The standard of medical facilities in Israel is reasonable.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: The Jewish Sabbath happens from sunset Friday until sunset Saturday.
In Orthodox neighbourhoods, driving or using a mobile phone or digital camera on the Sabbath may offend people.
Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Full travel advice: Local laws

sections.heading: Travel
added: All foreign nationals must have either an Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA-IL) or a visa before arriving in Israel.
To check your eligibility and to apply, visit the ETA-IL website (see 'Travel').
If you've been to the Democratic Republic of the Congo, South Sudan or Uganda in the preceding 21 days, you will be denied entry into Israel.
Flight cancellations and disruptions could occur with little or no notice, including due to airport and airspace closures.
GPS signals may be blocked in parts of Israel, disrupting navigational apps and devices.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: There are limits to what consular assistance we can provide in high-risk locations.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Tel Aviv or the Australian Embassy in Jordan.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy's social media accounts.
Full travel advice: Local contacts

sections.heading: Security situation
added: The situation in Israel and the Middle East region remains unpredictable.
There remains a risk of attacks and escalation.
We continue to advise do not travel to areas near the borders with Gaza (Palestine), Lebanon and Syria.

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:02 June 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution in The Bahamas due to the threat of violent crime. If you've been to the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Uganda or South Sudan within 30 days of arrival in The Bahamas, you may be subject to health screening and quarantine. Travellers may be victims of violent crime, including

sections.heading: Latest update
added: If you've been to the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Uganda or South Sudan within 30 days of arrival in The Bahamas, you may be subject to health screening and quarantine.
Travellers may be victims of violent crime, including in resorts.
Be aware of your surroundings and keep valuables out of sight (see 'Safety').
We advise: Exercise a high degree of caution in The Bahamas due to the threat of violent crime.
Download The Bahamas Download Americas

sections.heading: Safety
added: Armed robberies, burglaries, bag snatchings, theft, fraud and sexual assaults are the most common crimes committed against travellers in Freeport and Nassau.
Be aware of your surroundings, even in areas usually considered safe.
Keep valuables out of sight.
Sexual assault frequently occurs.
Don't visit deserted beaches or walk alone, especially after dark.

sections.heading: Health
added: HIV/AIDS infection rates are high.
Take precautions if you're engaging in high-risk activities.
Medical care is of a reasonable standard in Freeport and Nassau but limited elsewhere.
Medical expenses can be very high.
If you're severely ill or injured, you might need evacuation to the US.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences are severe.
They include fines and lengthy prison sentences.
Serious crimes can attract the death penalty, including aggravated murder, terrorism, treason and war crimes.
American authorities may conduct thorough drug searches in The Bahamas.

sections.heading: Travel
added: If you've been to the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Uganda or South Sudan within 30 days of arrival in The Bahamas you may be subject to health screening and quarantine.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of The Bahamas for the latest details.
Bahamian dollars (BSD) and US dollars are both accepted currencies.
You must declare if you enter or leave with BSD10,000 or more.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
You can get limited consular assistance from the Australian Honorary Consul based in Nassau and full consular help from the Australian High Commission in Trinidad and Tobago.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission’s social media accounts.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Armed robberies, burglaries, purse snatchings, theft, fraud and sexual assaults are the most common crimes committed against travellers in Freeport and Nassau, including in cruise ship terminals and resorts.
Avoid Nassau’s 'over the hill' area (south of Shirley Street), where gang violence occurs.
Criminals target travellers in Nassau and Freeport for: muggings armed robbery home invasion sexual assault The Bahamas has a high rate of murder and sexual assault.
Most murders happen at night in non-tourist areas in downtown Nassau.
Avoid these neighbourhoods.

sections.heading: Kidnapping
added: Kidnapping can happen anywhere, anytime, including in destinations that are typically at lower risk.
The Australian Government's longstanding policy is that it doesn't make payments or concessions to kidnappers.
More information: Kidnapping

added: Learn more: Measles COVID-19COVID-19 is an infectious viral disease.
Polio – WHO Temporary Recommendations Polio (poliomyelitis) is an infectious disease that can be prevented by vaccination.

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:06 July 2026 Latest update:We advise reconsider your need to travel overall in Uganda due to the outbreak of Ebola disease, as well as the threat of terrorism and violent crime and the risk of civil unrest. Higher levels also apply in some areas. There have been confirmed cases of Marburg virus disease in Kyegegwa District, Western Uganda. If you travel from U

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Higher levels also apply in some areas.
There have been confirmed cases of Marburg virus disease in Kyegegwa District, Western Uganda.
If you travel from Uganda, you may be subject to entry restrictions, health screening or quarantine in your transit and travel destinations.
Contact your airline directly for the latest updates and check the entry requirements of the destinations you're travelling to or transiting.
The border with the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) is currently closed (see 'Health' and 'Travel').

sections.heading: Safety
added: During periods of political tension, local authorities may restrict internet and telecommunications services, and increase security measures, restrict movement without notice and impose curfews.
Avoid large gatherings, monitor local media and follow the advice of authorities.
Terrorism is a threat in Uganda.
Attacks have caused many deaths and serious injuries.
Foreigners may be targeted.

sections.heading: Health
added: Outbreaks of Ebola disease, Marburg virus and mpox have occurred in parts of Africa, including in Uganda.
Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone displaying symptoms.
Yellow fever and malaria are common.
Other insect-borne diseases can occur.
Make sure your accommodation is insect proof.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Uganda prohibits same-sex relationships, and convictions may result in severe sentences.
Same-sex public displays of affection, like kissing in public places, could lead to arrest and imprisonment.
Members of the LGBTQIA+ community may face discrimination, potential violence and abuse.
LGBTQIA+ travellers should carefully consider the risks of travelling to Uganda.
Serious crimes can attract the death penalty or corporal punishment.

sections.heading: Travel
added: The East African Community's (EAC) Tourist Visa allows travellers multiple entries to Kenya, Rwanda and Uganda for 90 days.
You can also apply for a visa from the Government of Uganda.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
Contact the nearest high commission or consulate of Uganda for the latest details.
If you're travelling from Uganda, you may be subject to entry restrictions, health screening or quarantine in transit locations.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
The Australian Consulate in Kampala can provide limited consular assistance.
You can get full consular assistance from the Australian High Commission in Kenya.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorism remains a threat in Uganda.
Terrorists may target tourist areas or other places frequented by foreigners and where large crowds may gather.
Uganda has increased security arrangements in public places.
This includes borders and at the international airport.
Expect body, car and luggage checks when travelling, especially in Kampala.

sections.heading: Kidnapping
added: Kidnapping occurs across the world with political, ideological, and criminal motives.
Foreigners, including Australians, have been kidnapped overseas whilst travelling.
Kidnaps can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe.
There’s a risk of kidnapping in Uganda, including in the capital Kampala.
Kidnappings are usually criminally motivated and target the local population.

Avoid areas affected by protests, demonstrations or large public gatherings.

added: Fire and rescue services Call 115.

Latest update We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution in Angola overall due to the risk of civil unrest and the threat of violent crime.
Higher levels apply in some areas.
Protests and large gatherings can occur with little notice and may turn violent.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Avoid protests, demonstrations and large public gatherings.
Monitor media for updates and follow the advice of local authorities.
Authorities can restrict movements without notice.
Expect an increased security presence including around government buildings.
Angola has a high crime rate.

sections.heading: Health
added: Outbreaks of Ebola disease have occurred in parts of Africa.
Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone displaying symptoms.
Angola continues to experience a cholera outbreak affecting many Angolan provinces, including Luanda, Bengo, Icolo e Bengo, Cuanza Norte, Malanje and Benguela.
Be careful when consuming food and water.
HIV/AIDS infection rates are high.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: It's illegal to use cameras, binoculars or GPS devices near government infrastructure.
Take care when taking photos and using navigation apps and other equipment.
Don't take photos or film people without their permission, particularly around government buildings or key infrastructure.
Your equipment may be confiscated.
Angola recognises dual nationality.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Tourist can enter Angola without a visa.
For all other types of travel, you should apply for a visa online through Angola's Serviço de Migração.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
Contact the nearest Angolan embassy or consulatet for the latest information.
Being able to speak Portuguese is required for most interactions.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Consular Services Charter details what the Australian Government can and can't do to help you overseas.
For full consular assistance, contact the Australian High Commission in South Africa.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission’s social media accounts.

sections.heading: Violent crime
added: The crime rate in Angola is high.
Crimes are often violent.
Be aware and alert of your surroundings, particularly if you're out after dark.
Common crimes include: pickpocketing snatch and grab robberies armed robbery carjackings Attacks can occur at any time.
The risk of violent crime is much higher at night.

sections.heading: Latest update
added: There's an increase in methanol poisoning cases being reported.
Be alert to the potential risks of drink spiking and methanol poisoning when consuming alcoholic drinks (see 'Safety').
You'll need a visa to enter Brazil.
Arrange your visa before arrival, as you won’t be able to obtain a visa at the airport or border entry point.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice (see 'Travel').

sections.heading: Safety
added: There's an increase in methanol poisoning cases being reported.
Be alert to the potential risks of drink spiking and methanol poisoning when consuming alcoholic drinks.
See our advice on partying safely while overseas.
Violent crime, including mugging, armed robbery and carjacking is common.
These occur more often in large cities and during festivals, such as Carnival.

sections.heading: Health
added: Brazil experiences recurrent measles outbreaks.
It is important to get vaccinated.
Common insect-borne diseases include dengue, malaria, yellow fever and zika virus.
Protect yourself from insect bites.
Get vaccinated against yellow fever where possible.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences are severe and include long prison sentences.
You must carry proof of identity with you.
Always carry photo ID, such as a driver's licence and a photocopy of your passport.
Dual nationals must enter Brazil on their Brazilian passport.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You need a visa to enter Brazil.
You won’t be able to obtain a visa at the airport or border entry point.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
For more information, contact an embassy or consulate of Brazil.
Brazilian children (including dual nationals) must have written consent from any non-accompanying parent to depart Brazil.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Consular Services Charter details what the Australian Government can and can't do to help you overseas.
Australian's Honorary Consulate in Rio de Janeiro provides limited consular services.
For full consular assistance, contact the Australian Embassy in Brasilia or the Australian Consulate-General in São Paulo.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Violent crime, often involving weapons, is common, especially in large cities.
Most crime is opportunistic.
Common examples which involve tourists include the theft of valuables at the beach or from bags and valuables snatched while sightseeing.
Tourists have also been robbed at knife or gunpoint.
Local authorities advise against taking valuables and passports to the beach.

sections.heading: Scams
added: Scammers take advantage of people looking for romantic partners, often via dating websites, apps or social media, by pretending to be prospective companions.
Be wary of any online invitation you receive to travel to an unfamiliar location, especially if the location is changed at late notice and if someone is offering romance.
The scammer may kidnap you and force you to transfer or withdraw money and then extort your loved ones for more money to secure your release.
Take caution when purchasing food and beverages from vendors, especially at the beach when paying by card.
Confirm the price before and check the figure on the card machine is correct and hasn't been inflated.

Land border crossings between Cambodia and Thailand remain closed.

You can apply for an e-visa online or get a tourist visa on arrival.
These are valid for 30 days.

sections.heading: Medical emergencies
added: Call 119 in Phnom Penh or go to a hospital.
Outside of Phnom Penh you will need to contact a hospital directly.

sections.heading: Safety
added: We advise do not travel to areas within 50 kilometres of the Cambodia-Thailand border in the provinces of Banteay Meanchey, Oddar Meanchey, Preah Vihear, Battambang, Pailin, Pursat and Koh Kong due to the risk of armed conflict between Cambodian and Thai force
There have been military strikes and violence, and the ongoing presence of landmines and other explosive remnants of war.
We advise exercise a high degree of caution overall due to recent armed conflict and related security risks.
The security situation remains unpredictable.
Check local and international media for updates, follow the instructions of local authorities and pay close attention to your personal security.

sections.heading: Health
added: There have been reports of fatal cases of avian influenza (H5N1) in Cambodia.
Avoid close contact with birds and animals that might be infected, especially dead or sick birds, and avoid places like poultry or dairy farms and markets that sell live animals.
Take care buying medication in Cambodia.
Local pharmacies may sell fake medication.
Carry enough medication from Australia for your trip.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don’t use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences are severe.
They include long prison sentences, even for small amounts.
Laws related to incitement and defamation can be broadly defined and applied.

Be aware that some activities that could be considered the normal exercise of freedom of expression and association could be deemed by authorities in Cambodia to constitute crimes, even if they wouldn't in Australia.

sections.heading: Travel

This situation can change at short notice.

You need a visa to enter Cambodia.
You can apply for an e-visa online or get a tourist visa on arrival.

sections.heading: Local contacts

sections.heading: Health
added: Common insect-borne diseases include leishmaniasis and typhus.
Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof.
Waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases are common.
Boil drinking water or drink bottled water.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Morocco has the death penalty for serious crimes, including high treason, terrorism, multiple murder and rape.
Same-sex relationships and sex outside marriage are illegal.
You may not be allowed to share the same hotel room with members of the opposite sex if you're unmarried or unrelated.
This is more likely where at least one person is Moroccan.
Understand and follow local laws.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Australians generally don't need a visa if travelling to Morocco for less than 90 days.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact your nearest Moroccan Embassy for the latest information.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.

sections.heading: Terrorism
added: Terrorism is a threat worldwide.

Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places.
Past attacks targeted locations that are popular with tourists.
You could get caught up in attacks directed at others.

sections.heading: Demonstrations and protests

sections.heading: Safety
added: We continue to advise do not travel to Ukraine due to the volatile security environment and ongoing military conflict.
If you’re in Ukraine, you should leave as soon as possible if it’s safe to do so.
The security situation could deteriorate with little warning, posing a serious risk to life.
The Russian invasion of Ukraine is ongoing.
Heavy fighting is occurring in parts of eastern and southern Ukraine.

sections.heading: Health
added: Take steps to reduce your risk of exposure to the virus.
Infectious diseases include polio, tuberculosis, measles, diphtheria, hepatitis, and rabies.
Drink only boiled or bottled water.

sections.heading: Travel
added: The security situation continues to be volatile.

sections.heading: Local contacts

Subway stations may be available as shelters.
Be alert and aware of your surroundings.
