Avoid using public transport or taxis.

sections.heading: Health
added: Yellow fever, malaria and other insect-borne diseases are common.
Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof.
Get vaccinated before you travel and consider taking anti-malarial medication.
The HIV/AIDS infection rate is high.
Take precautions if you engage in high-risk activities.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences include mandatory jail terms.
It's illegal to export precious minerals without a licence from the Ministry of Mines and Resources.
Be careful when taking photos.
It's illegal to take pictures or videos of government buildings, airports or bridges.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You need a visa to enter Sierra Leone.
If you're entering or leaving Sierra Leone, you must complete an online health declaration before travel using the Sierra Leone Health Travel Portal.
You may also be subject to health screening on arrival.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact the nearest embassy or consulate of Sierra Leone for the latest details.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Sierra Leone.
You can get consular help from the Australian High Commission in Ghana.

sections.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
added: Civil unrest, demonstrations, public protests and events that draw large numbers of people can turn violent.
Clashes between protestors and security forces have resulted in serious injuries and casualties in the past.
Avoid demonstrations, protests and areas where large gatherings are taking place.
The security environment in Sierra Leone remains fluid.
Nationwide curfews, checkpoints and additional security measures could be implemented at short notice.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Crime levels are high in Sierra Leone, including in Freetown.

Common crimes include: armed robbery violent assault carjacking pickpocketing and mugging bag snatching Pickpocketing and purse snatching is common on the ferry to and from Lungi International Airport, near beach resorts and in bars, restaurants and nightclubs
Security risks increase at night.
Some criminals target travellers and foreigners.

To stay safe: watch your personal belongings and avoiding displaying signs of personal wealth avoid walking in isolated areas or going out after dark lock your accommodation, even when you're in it arrange your transport in advance avoid using public transport

Get a visa before you arrive. Don't overstay your visa as there are penalties (see 'Travel'). Crossing the Turkmenistan land border requires a special authorisation from the Government of Turkmenistan. Borders can open and close at short noti

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Get a visa before you arrive.
Don't overstay your visa as there are penalties (see 'Travel').
Crossing the Turkmenistan land border requires a special authorisation from the Government of Turkmenistan.
Borders can open and close at short notice.
Entry and exit requirements can change without notice.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Pickpocketing, mugging and theft occurs.
The risk is higher on trains and at markets.
Avoid going out alone, especially at night.
Criminals target travellers using unofficial taxis.
Always book a licensed taxi.

sections.heading: Health
added: Some sleeping tablets and medications containing codeine may be restricted.
Check legal limits before you travel and declare all prescription medication and other restricted items on arrival.
Infectious diseases include cholera, hepatitis, measles, tuberculosis and typhoid.
Drink only boiled or bottled water.
Avoid raw or undercooked food.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: You must always carry ID.
Security officials often ask for proof of identity.
Ask for identification if you are unsure the person is an official.
Penalties for drug offences are severe and can include long prison sentences.
It's illegal to have same sex relationships.

sections.heading: Travel
added: For information on crossing the border into Turkmenistan, see Travel.
You need a letter of invitation from the State Migration Service of Turkmenistan to apply for a visa, even if you're only transiting.
You must keep the letter of invitation with you when you are in Turkmenistan.
Get a visa before you arrive.
There are penalties for overstaying your visa.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Australia doesn't have an embassy or consulate in Turkmenistan.
Contact the Australian Embassy in Russia for consular help.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Crime in Turkmenistan includes: pickpocketing and theft mugging The risk is higher: on trains, particularly overnight services at markets People travelling in unofficial taxis have been robbed.
The risk of crime is also higher at night.
Women are at higher risk.

To protect yourself from crime, including in taxis: avoid travelling or walking alone, especially at night avoid isolated areas pay attention to your surroundings avoid using public transport keep valuables out of sight ask staff at hotels, restaurants or plac
Ask for identification if you are unsure the person is an official.

sections.heading: Kidnapping
added: Kidnapping can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe.
The Australian Government’s longstanding policy is that it doesn’t make payments or concessions to kidnappers.
More information: Kidnapping

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:25 June 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise exercise normal safety precautions in Mauritius. The Government of Mauritius has imposed border measures in response to the Ebola disease outbreak. These include restrictions on entry and health screening checks for travellers who have recently visited the Democratic Republic of Congo, Uganda or South Sudan. Che

sections.heading: Latest update
added: The Government of Mauritius has imposed border measures in response to the Ebola disease outbreak.
These include restrictions on entry and health screening checks for travellers who have recently visited the Democratic Republic of Congo, Uganda or South Sudan.
Check the latest requirements with your airline or travel provider before departure (see 'Travel').
We advise: Exercise normal safety precautions in Mauritius.
Download Mauritius Download Africa

sections.heading: Medical emergencies
added: Call 114 or 999 for Service Aide Medicale d'Urgence, a free state-run ambulance and emergency service.
Call 118 for Darne private clinic.
Call 114 or 999 for Service Aide Medicale d'Urgence, the public ambulance and emergency service.
If you would prefer the ambulance to take you to a private hospital, you will need to contact that hospital to collect you.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Cyclone season is from November to May, but cyclones can also occur at other times.
Cyclones can cause heavy rain, strong winds and flash flooding.
There may be disruptions to power and water supplies.
Monitor official sources and follow the advice of local authorities.
Civil unrest is rare, but demonstrations can happen.

sections.heading: Health
added: Insect-borne diseases including dengue and chikungunya occur in Mauritius.
Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof.
Foodborne, waterborne and other infectious diseases are a risk.
Drink boiled or bottled water.
Avoid raw or undercooked food.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Know and follow local laws.
You can’t smoke or vape in any indoor public area and some outdoor places.
It's illegal to own cigarette papers or rolling machines.
Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences include prison sentences and heavy fines.

sections.heading: Travel
added: The Government of Mauritius has imposed border measures in response to the Ebola disease outbreak.
These include restrictions on entry and health screening checks for travellers who have recently visited the Democratic Republic of Congo, Uganda or South Sudan.
Check the latest requirements with your airline or travel provider before departure (see 'Travel' and 'Health' sections in Full Advice below of more information).
If you're staying for less than 60 days, you can get a 'free of charge visa' on arrival.
You must apply for any other visa types before you arrive.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular assistance, contact the Australian High Commission in Mauritius.
The High Commission is open to the public for passport, consular and notarial services via appointment only.

sections.heading: Crime
added: Crime levels in Mauritius are low.
However, petty crime and opportunistic theft occurs, including pickpocketing and bag snatching.
This is most likely to occur in crowded outdoor shopping areas, tourist areas, such as beaches, and near ATMs.
There have also been cases of theft at unregistered beachside bungalows.
The crime rate is higher in downtown Port Louis and in the coastal tourist centres of Grand Bay, Pereybere and Flic en Flac.

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:17 June 2026 Latest update:We've reviewed our travel advice for United Arab Emirates (UAE) and have lowered the overall travel advice level. We now advise reconsider your need to travel due to the unpredictable security situation in the UAE and the region. There remains a risk of military action. Follow local warning systems and, if warned of an imminent attac

sections.heading: Latest update
added: We now advise reconsider your need to travel due to the unpredictable security situation in the UAE and the region.
There remains a risk of military action.
Follow local warning systems and, if warned of an imminent attack, move to an enclosed hardened shelter.
If these are not available, seek an inner room without windows.
Monitor developments closely and follow the advice of local authorities.

sections.heading: Middle East conflict
added: The security situation in the Middle East region remains unpredictable.
Military conflict in the region may cause travel disruptions and your travel plans may be affected.
‘Reconsider your need to travel’ also means ‘reconsider your need to transit’.
If you need to travel to any level 3 locations, stay as short a time as possible and eliminate unnecessary activities.
Advice levels can go up at short notice if conditions deteriorate.

sections.heading: Safety
added: We've reviewed our travel advice for the UAE and have lowered the overall travel advice level.
We now advise 'reconsider your need to travel' overall due to the unpredictable security situation in the UAE and the region.
There remains a risk of military action.
If this happens, prioritise your safety.
Follow local warning systems and, if warned of an imminent attack, move to an enclosed hardened shelter.

sections.heading: Health
added: Be prepared for extreme weather conditions.
From June to September, temperatures can exceed 50°C, and sand and dust storms are common.
During periods of extreme heat, it is important to stay out of the sun and drink plenty of water.
Intense storms can occur between December and May and may lead to flash flooding.
Monitor local media and follow the advice of local authorities.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Many things that are acceptable in Australia are illegal in the UAE.
It's illegal to insult an ethnic or religious group, the UAE, the royal families/government officials or the UAE flag.
Same-sex relationships are illegal and same-sex marriage is not recognised.
Familiarise yourself with local rules before you go.
You can be fined, jailed or deported if you take photos or videos of military conflict, government buildings, embassies, palaces, airports and transport infrastructure.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Commercial flights are available to depart subject to airspace being open.
The UAE airspace may open or close at short notice, impacting flights at Abu Dhabi and Dubai International airports.
The UAE is restricting entry for travellers who have recently been in the Ebola-affected countries, unless they've been outside these countries for more than 21 days.
These measures apply to all travellers who have been in any Ebola-affected country, even if they have travelled through other locations on the way to the UAE.
Travellers from Ebola-affected regions may also face health screening, quarantine or other entry requirements in transit or destination countries.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
If you need emergency consular assistance, contact the Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305 (if you're overseas) and 1300 555 135 (in Australia).
To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy's social media accounts.

sections.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
added: Demonstrations in the UAE are rare and must be authorised by the government.
Wearing clothing reminiscent of a political cause may be seen as a form of protest.
Australians have been detained for wearing the Palestinian Keffiyeh (head scarf) and rainbow colours.

To protect yourself in case of unrest: avoid all rallies and protests monitor the media for news of possible demonstrations or protests be prepared to change your travel plans More information: Protests and civil unrest

changes.updateNote: Still current at:09 July 2026 Updated:05 June 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise exercise normal safety precautions in Laos overall. Higher levels apply in some areas. Several foreign nationals, including Australians, died of methanol poisoning through consuming alcoholic drinks in 2024. Be alert to the potential risks, particularly with spirit-based drinks, including cocktails. For further

sections.heading: Latest update
added: Higher levels apply in some areas.
Several foreign nationals, including Australians, died of methanol poisoning through consuming alcoholic drinks in 2024.
Be alert to the potential risks, particularly with spirit-based drinks, including cocktails.
For further advice on the risks around methanol poisoning as well as drink spiking (see 'Safety').
Lao authorities issued an order prohibiting the sale and consumption of Tiger Vodka and Tiger Whisky due to their concerns about these products being a health risk.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Several foreign nationals, including Australians, died of methanol poisoning through consuming alcoholic drinks in 2024.
Be alert to the potential risks around drink spiking and methanol poisoning through consuming alcoholic drinks.
Lao authorities issued an order prohibiting the sale and consumption of Tiger Vodka and Tiger Whisky due to their concerns about these products being a health risk.
Australians should avoid consuming these products.
Some restaurants sell food and drink containing potentially harmful drugs.

sections.heading: Health
added: The standard of medical facilities is generally poor.
Medical evacuation to another country will be difficult.
Malaria is a risk in southern provinces.
Dengue is a risk everywhere in Laos, especially in the wet season from May to November.
Other insect-borne diseases include Zika virus and Japanese encephalitis.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Severe penalties for drug offences include the death sentence.
Intimate relationships between Lao citizens and foreigners who aren't married are illegal.
This includes sexual relationships and living together.
Marriages between Lao citizens and foreigners must be pre-approved.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Laos has launched a new digital system for filling in immigration arrival and departure information.

You'll have the option of continuing to fill in the paper immigration form or you can submit this information through the Laos Digital Immigration Form (LDIF) on the Department of Immigration of Lao PDR website, which is being trialled to streamline border pro
If you use the LDIF, you must complete this within three days before entering or exiting Laos PDR.
You can obtain visas-on-arrival and e-visas.
Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Consular Services Charter details what we can and can't do to help you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Vientiane.
To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy’s social media accounts Full travel advice: Local contacts

sections.heading: Crime
added: Violent opportunistic crime such as robbery occurs in Laos, including in: Vientiane Vang Vieng Luang Prabang Bokeo Province along the border with Myanmar, Thailand and around the Golden Triangle Special Economic Zone (GTSEZ).

sections.heading: Petty crime
added: Petty crime is common, especially in tourist areas.
It may include: thieves on motorbikes snatching bags theft from guesthouses In the lead-up to local festivals, there's an increase in theft and violent crime.
This includes Lao New Year in April.
Thieves often operate in pairs, with one person creating a distraction while the other steals unguarded items.

Thieves sometimes target foreigners in rural and remote locations, especially along: Route 6 (near the town of Sam Neua, Huaphan Province) Route 7 (Phou Khoun to Phonsavanh) To protect yourself against petty crime: carry only what you need — leave valuables in

We now advise exercise a high degree of caution in Jordan due to the threat of terrorism and the unpredictable security situation in the region. Higher levels apply in some areas. If the regional security situation deteriorates again, this may result in airsp

sections.heading: Latest update
added: We now advise exercise a high degree of caution in Jordan due to the threat of terrorism and the unpredictable security situation in the region.
Higher levels apply in some areas.
If the regional security situation deteriorates again, this may result in airspace closures, flight cancellations and other travel disruptions at short notice.
Check with your airline or tour operator before travel.
Monitor developments closely and follow the advice of local authorities.

sections.heading: Middle East conflict
added: The security situation in the Middle East region remains unpredictable.
Military conflict in the region may cause travel disruptions and your travel plans may be affected.
‘Reconsider your need to travel’ also means ‘reconsider your need to transit’.
If you need to travel to any level 3 locations, stay as short a time as possible and eliminate unnecessary activities.
Advice levels can go up at short notice if conditions deteriorate.

sections.heading: Safety
added: The situation in the Middle East region remains unpredictable.
If the regional security situation deteriorates again, this may result in airspace closures, flight cancellations and other travel disruptions at short notice.
Check with your airline or tour operator before travel.
Monitor developments closely and follow the advice of local authorities.
Stay aware of your environment.

sections.heading: Health
added: There are reports of cases of Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus (MERS-CoV).
Avoid consuming products contaminated with camel secretions.
Wash hands regularly before and after touching animals.
Waterborne, foodborne, insect-borne and other infectious diseases are prevalent.
They include hepatitis, typhoid, measles and sandfly fever.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Many laws differ from laws in Australia, including sex outside marriage, photos of embassies, military or security sites and religious activities.
Familiarise yourself before you go.
Get professional advice for local legal matters.
You may be prevented from leaving the country until issues are resolved.
Don't use or carry illegal drugs.

sections.heading: Travel
added: You'll need a visa to visit Jordan.
Multiple visa options are available on the Ministry of Interior E-Services Site webpage, depending on your travel requirements.
If you're visiting Jordan on an e-Visa, a single visa is valid for 90 days.
Visitors are required to register their residency details at the immigration desk on arrival at the airport.
If you're visiting for tourism and staying for at least 3 nights, you can purchase a Jordan Pass before you arrive.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Amman.
To stay up to date with local information follow the Australian Embassy Jordan on social media.

sections.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
added: Demonstrations and protests occur frequently, including in Amman and regional centres.
They're prompted by: domestic, regional and international events political or economic issues politically significant anniversaries Most have been peaceful.
However, public protests and events that draw large groups of people can turn violent.
Demonstrations often occur after Friday noon prayers but can happen at any time.
These often result in road closures.

sections.heading: Local emergency contacts
added: Fire and rescue services Call 911 or 118.
Medical emergencies Call 911 or 128.
Latest update We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution in Costa Rica.
Violent crime, including carjackings and 'express kidnappings', is increasingly common.
Be aware of your surroundings.

sections.heading: Safety
added: Violent crime, including carjackings and 'express kidnappings', is increasingly common.
Criminals often target tourist areas, resorts, transport hubs and public transport.
Be aware of your surroundings.
Plan to arrive or leave the airport during daylight hours.
Avoid carrying valuables where possible.

sections.heading: Health
added: Malaria is a serious risk in Limon and Puntarenas, including near the border with Panama.
Consider taking anti-malarial medication if you're travelling to these areas.
Zika virus is widespread.
If you're pregnant, discuss your travel plans with your doctor.
The risk of mosquito-borne illnesses increases during the wet season from April to November.

sections.heading: Local laws
added: Don't use or carry illegal drugs.
Penalties for drug offences are severe.
They include long prison sentences.
Officials may arrest you if you're with someone who's using drugs.
Same-sex relationships are legal, but they aren't socially accepted in all areas.

sections.heading: Travel
added: Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice.
You should contact your nearest Embassy of Costa Rica for the latest details or check with your airline or travel provider.
If you travel via the US, ensure you meet US entry or transit requirements.
Check travel requirements for children.
You may need extra documents if only one parent or guardian is travelling with a child.

sections.heading: Local contacts
added: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance.
There are limits to what we can do.
Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas.
Australia has a consulate in San José, headed by an honorary consul.
This consulate provides limited assistance to Australians in Costa Rica.

sections.heading: Petty crime
added: Petty crime is common, such as pickpocketing and bag-snatching.

Take extra care of your valuables in: airports bus terminals public transport To prevent petty crime: be alert after dark avoid being on the streets alone pay attention in crowded areas use ATMs in daylight hours withdraw or change money in secure areas like h

changes.updateNote: New information about Typhoon Bavi ('Warnings and insurance' page).

sections.heading: Typhoon Bavi
added: Typhoon Bavi is expected to have its strongest impact on Taiwan from 10 July to 11 July.
The Central Weather Administration is forecasting heavy to torrential rain, strong winds, rough seas and possible transport disruptions.
The greatest rainfall risk is for central and northern Taiwan.
Flash flooding and landslides are possible, particularly in mountainous areas, while coastal areas may experience large waves.
Monitor official weather warnings, check transport schedules, and follow the advice of local authorities.

sections.heading: Typhoon Bavi
added: Typhoon Bavi is expected to pass northeast of Taiwan, affecting the northern part of the island, including Taipei, on July 10.
The storm is likely to bring excessive rainfall and violent winds.

changes.updateNote: Information about wildfires ('Warnings and insurance' page).

sections.heading: Wildfires
added: Wildfires can occur across France during the summer months.
The risk is highest in the south of the country, particularly along the Mediterranean coast, in Corsica, and in areas of the south-west during periods of hot, dry weather.
Check the French weather service’s fire alert map before travelling and monitor local authority advice during your stay.
Conditions can change quickly and access to forests, countryside areas and tourist sites may be restricted at short notice.

sections.heading: Forest and maquis fires
added: Forest and maquis fires often occur in summer, particularly in the south of the country and in Corsica.
In recent years, due to high heat and drought conditions, the forest fire season has lengthened and broadened to cover the entire country.
The risk of major fires has also increased.

changes.updateNote: New information about recent cases of drug-assisted robbery and sexual assault (see ‘Drug assisted robbery and sexual assault’ on the ‘Safety and security’ page).

sections.heading: Drug assisted robbery and sexual assault
added: There have been reports of criminals in Trinidad and Tobago using drugs, deception and sexual solicitation, including via dating apps, to facilitate robbery and sexual assault.
Victims may be lured and drugged or beaten and robbed.
Incidents have occurred in bars, nightclubs, hotels and on city streets, sometimes involving groups working together.

You should: avoid accepting invitations from strangers or inviting them to your accommodation take care when using dating apps keep food and drinks under supervision plan travel routes in advance, particularly at night If drugged, seek local medical attention
